prompt.question:
Which of these is the one being described below?

In Spring’s web support, this annotation indicates that a method parameter is bound to a URI template variable.
feedbacks: Incorrect. This annotation is used to bind the HTTP request body to a method parameter, not a URI template variable.
Incorrect. This annotation is used to map web requests to specific handler classes or methods but does not bind method parameters to URI variables.
Incorrect. This is an enumeration (not an annotation) used within @RequestMapping to specify the HTTP method (GET, POST, etc.) that a method should handle.
Correct. This annotation is specifically used in Spring MVC to bind a method parameter to a URI template variable in the request path.
prompt.explanation:
Binding URI Template Variables with @PathVariable

In Spring MVC, @PathVariable is an essential annotation that allows you to extract values from the URI path and bind them directly to method parameters in your controller. This is commonly used in RESTful APIs where parts of the resource path are dynamic (such as IDs or names).

Example Usage:

@RestController
public class UserController {
    @GetMapping("/users/{id}")
    public User getUserById(@PathVariable("id") Long id) {
        // The 'id' parameter is automatically populated from the URL
        return userService.findUserById(id);
    }
}

Here, if you request /users/42, the method parameter id will automatically receive the value 42.

Best Practices:

  • Always validate or sanitize the values you get via @PathVariable to prevent security issues.

  • Use descriptive variable names in your URI patterns to increase code readability.

  • You can omit the value in @PathVariable if the method parameter name matches the URI template variable:

    @GetMapping("/users/{id}")
public User getUserById(@PathVariable Long id) { ... }

Related Annotations:

  • @RequestParam is used for query parameters (e.g., /users?id=42).

  • @RequestBody is used to bind the request body to a method parameter, often with POST/PUT.

results.question_plain: Which of these is the one being described below?In Spring’s web support, this annotation indicates that a method parameter is bound to a URI template variable.

prompt.question:
The following code is not a valid configuration class, why?   


// in a separate file - Person.java 
public class Person {
}

// in a separate file - PersonsConfiguration.java 
@Configuration
public class PersonsConfiguration {
    @Bean
    private Person createPerson() {
        return new Person();
    }

    @Bean
    public Person createPerson2() {
        return new Person();
    }
}
feedbacks: Incorrect. @Configuration is a valid Spring annotation used to indicate that the class declares one or more @Bean methods. @ApplicationContext is not an annotation but rather an interface in Spring for accessing the application context. There is no such requirement to use @ApplicationContext instead of @Configuration.

Incorrect. @Bean is a method-level annotation and is used to define beans in configuration classes. It is not applied at the class level, and using it on a method (as shown in the code) is correct in general.

Correct. Methods annotated with @Bean must be public or at least package-private. If a method is private, Spring will not be able to proxy or call the method to instantiate the bean, and the bean will not be registered. This is why the code with private Person createPerson() is not valid as a Spring bean definition.

Incorrect. It's perfectly valid to have multiple @Bean methods returning the same type, as long as they have different bean names (which they do here: createPerson and createPerson2). Spring will register both as different beans in the application context.
prompt.explanation:
Rules for Defining @Bean Methods in @Configuration Classes

In Spring, configuration classes annotated with @Configuration are used to define beans using methods marked with the @Bean annotation. These beans are then managed by the Spring container.

Key Rules for @Bean Methods:

  • The method must not be private. If it is, Spring cannot access it to create and manage the bean. Typically, @Bean methods are public, but they can also be package-private or protected.

  • Method names are used as bean names by default. If you want to override this, you can provide a name in the annotation: @Bean("myBeanName").

  • Multiple @Bean methods can return the same type. Each will be registered as a separate bean, identified by its method (bean) name.

  • The configuration class itself should be a top-level class (not a nested or inner class) and must be annotated with @Configuration.

Example – Correct Configuration:

@Configuration
public class PersonsConfiguration {

    @Bean
    public Person createPerson() {
        return new Person();
    }

    @Bean
    public Person createPerson2() {
        return new Person();
    }
}

Both beans will be available in the application context: one as createPerson and one as createPerson2.

Common Pitfalls:

  • Marking a @Bean method as private will prevent Spring from managing it.

  • Using @Bean at the class level is incorrect; it must be on methods.

  • Confusion between @Configuration and other Spring annotations can lead to incorrect usage.

Practical Tip:
Always keep your @Bean methods at least package-private. It's good practice to keep them public for clarity and compatibility.
answers: @Configuration is not a valid annotation, we must use @ApplicationContext
@Bean is a class-level annotation, thus we will get a compilation error
Methods annotated with @Bean must not be private
One configuration class can't have two @Bean annotated methods returning the same type

prompt.question: Which of the following concepts in Spring allows configuring settings such as 'required', 'requires new', and 'mandatory' to define transaction behavior?
feedbacks: Incorrect. The transaction manager is responsible for managing transactions (begin, commit, rollback), but it does not define propagation behaviors like 'required', 'requires new', or 'mandatory'. It is a lower-level infrastructure component.

Correct. Transaction propagation refers to how transactions relate to each other when calling methods within the same or different transactions. Settings like REQUIRED, REQUIRES_NEW, and MANDATORY are all propagation behaviors that can be configured using Spring’s @Transactional annotation.

Incorrect. The isolation level determines how transaction integrity is visibly affected by other concurrent transactions (e.g., READ_COMMITTED, SERIALIZABLE). It does not control propagation behavior.
Incorrect. A transaction proxy is a mechanism used internally by Spring to apply transactional behavior to beans, but it does not define the semantics of propagation itself.
prompt.explanation:
Transaction Propagation in Spring

Transaction propagation in Spring controls how transactional methods interact when one method calls another. This determines whether a new transaction should be started, an existing one should be joined, or if a method must always run within a transaction. These behaviors are set using the propagation attribute of the @Transactional annotation.

Common Propagation Behaviors:

  • REQUIRED (default): If a transaction exists, join it; otherwise, start a new one.

  • REQUIRES_NEW: Always start a new transaction, suspending any existing one.

  • MANDATORY: Must run within an existing transaction; otherwise, an exception is thrown.

Example:

@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRED)
public void doSomething() {
    // will join an existing transaction or create a new one
}

@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)
public void doSomethingElse() {
    // always runs in a new transaction
}

Practical Tips:

  • Use REQUIRED for most business methods.

  • Use REQUIRES_NEW when a method’s transaction must be independent.

  • Always document your transaction boundaries clearly to avoid unexpected behavior.

Best Practice:
Clearly understand how your service methods interact and choose the propagation behavior that matches your business requirements to avoid data inconsistencies and transaction issues.

results.question_plain: Which of the following concepts in Spring allows configuring settings such as 'required', 'requires new', and 'mandatory' to define transaction behavior?

prompt.question: In the Spring Framework, what accurately describes the behavior of a bean with the 'prototype' scope?
feedbacks: Incorrect. Beans with prototype scope are not shared across contexts; each request creates a new instance within a single application context.

Correct. Spring creates a new instance of a prototype-scoped bean each time it is requested, and after instantiation, the container does not manage the bean’s complete lifecycle (such as destruction callbacks).
Incorrect. Prototype-scoped beans are not meant to be shared; they are suitable for stateful objects where a new instance is needed for each request.
Incorrect. The default scope for all Spring beans is singleton, not prototype.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring Framework, bean scopes define how and when the beans are created, as well as their lifecycle within the container. The two most common scopes are singleton and prototype. Singleton scope (default) means only one instance of the bean is created per Spring container, and this instance is shared. In contrast, the prototype scope means a new instance of the bean is created every time it is requested from the container (via getBean()), making it ideal for stateful objects or cases where multiple instances with different internal states are required.

When a bean is defined with the prototype scope, Spring manages its instantiation and initialization, but it does not handle the full lifecycle: for example, any destruction callbacks specified with @PreDestroy or the DisposableBean interface are not called automatically. This means the client code is responsible for cleaning up any resources.

Best practice: Use prototype scope for beans that maintain state specific to a single use or client, such as form backing objects, and be cautious with dependency injection—injecting a prototype bean into a singleton can lead to undesired sharing of the prototype bean unless handled correctly (e.g., using method injection or ObjectFactory).
Example:

@Component
@Scope("prototype")
public class Task {
    // Task-specific state here
}

When you request this bean multiple times, Spring will return a new Task instance each time.
answers: Beans with prototype scope are shared across multiple application contexts.
A new instance of a prototype-scoped bean is created each time it is requested, and Spring does not reuse the previously created instance.
Prototype-scoped beans are suitable for objects that are stateful and need to be shared among multiple components.
The prototype scope is the default scope for all Spring beans.

results.question_plain: In the Spring Framework, what accurately describes the behavior of a bean with the 'prototype' scope?

prompt.question: Which of the following methods in JdbcTemplate is recommended for DDL operations?
feedbacks: This method is not correct because it is used to execute a SQL query that returns multiple rows, mapping each row to a Map, which is intended for SELECT statements, not DDL operations.
This method is not correct because, while it can be used for DML statements like INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE, it is not specifically intended for DDL operations such as CREATE or DROP.
This method is not correct because it is used for executing SELECT queries and mapping results, so it is not suitable for DDL operations.
This is the correct answer because the execute method in JdbcTemplate is designed for running SQL statements where you do not expect any result (such as DDL statements: CREATE, ALTER, DROP).
prompt.explanation:
Overview

In Spring’s JDBC module, the JdbcTemplate class simplifies database operations by handling resource management and error handling for you. When working with databases, you often need to execute Data Definition Language (DDL) operations like CREATE TABLE, ALTER TABLE, or DROP TABLE. These operations modify the database schema rather than interact with row data.

Recommended Method for DDL

For DDL operations in Spring, the execute method of JdbcTemplate is specifically designed for SQL statements that do not return any result set. This is ideal for running SQL that modifies schema, such as table creation or deletion, where you do not expect a result or need to map any output.

Example Usage

@Autowired
private JdbcTemplate jdbcTemplate;

// Create a table
jdbcTemplate.execute("CREATE TABLE employees (id INT PRIMARY KEY, name VARCHAR(100))");

// Drop a table
jdbcTemplate.execute("DROP TABLE IF EXISTS employees");

Why Not Use update, query, or queryForList?

  • update is meant for modifying data (INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE), not schema.

  • query and queryForList are for retrieving data (SELECT statements).

  • Only execute provides a clean and intention-revealing way to run arbitrary SQL, especially for DDL.

Best Practice

  • Always use execute for DDL: This avoids confusion in your codebase and makes the intention clear for future maintainers.

  • Keep schema changes in migrations: For real applications, use migration tools like Flyway or Liquibase for managing schema changes rather than ad-hoc execution, but execute is useful for programmatic or dynamic DDL tasks.

results.question_plain: Which of the following methods in JdbcTemplate is recommended for DDL operations?

prompt.question: Which of the following transaction propagation behaviour will create a new transaction if one does not already exist?
feedbacks: This propagation does not create a new transaction. If a transaction already exists, it will participate in it; otherwise, it will execute non-transactionally.
This propagation will throw an exception if there is no existing transaction; it never creates a new one.
This is the correct answer. If a transaction exists, the method will join it. If there is no transaction, a new one will be created.

If there is no existing transaction, it behaves like REQUIRED and creates a new transaction. However, the question asks specifically about propagation designed primarily for creating transactions when none exists, which is REQUIRED's main purpose. NESTED's main purpose is creating nested/savepoint transactions within existing ones.
prompt.explanation:
Transaction propagation determines how transactional methods interact with existing transactions. When a method annotated with @Transactional is called, Spring checks if there is an existing transaction and decides what to do based on the specified propagation behavior.

Common Propagation Types:

  • REQUIRED: This is the default propagation type. If there’s already a transaction, the method will run within it. If there isn’t one, Spring starts a new transaction. This ensures that transactional work is always executed inside a transaction context.

  • SUPPORTS: The method will join the current transaction if one exists. If not, it will run without a transaction. It never creates a transaction on its own.

  • MANDATORY: The method must run within an existing transaction. If none exists, an exception (TransactionRequiredException) is thrown.

  • NESTED: If a transaction exists, the method runs in a nested transaction (using a savepoint). If not, it behaves like REQUIRED and creates a new transaction. Nested transactions allow partial rollbacks to a savepoint without affecting the outer transaction.

Practical Example

@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRED)
public void doWork() {
    // Will join existing transaction or start a new one
}

@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.SUPPORTS)
public void readOnlyWork() {
    // Will join existing transaction or run non-transactionally
}

Best Practices

  • Use REQUIRED for most business service methods where you want to guarantee work is transactional.

  • Use SUPPORTS for methods that can run either inside or outside a transaction (typically for read-only operations).

  • Use MANDATORY if you want to ensure a transaction is always active (useful for internal API contracts).

  • Use NESTED only when you specifically need savepoint-based rollback functionality, and your transaction manager supports it (e.g., DataSourceTransactionManager with JDBC).

results.question_plain: Which of the following transaction propagation behaviour will create a new transaction if one does not already exist?

prompt.question: Which of the following factors influence the configuration setup performed by Spring Boot?
feedbacks: This answer is correct. Spring Boot automatically loads settings from external configuration files, which influence the application's configuration, such as ports, database URLs, and feature toggles.
This is correct. Spring Boot uses classpath scanning to enable or disable auto-configurations based on the presence of certain dependencies, like web servers or database connectors.
This is correct. JVM arguments, such as -Dproperty=value, can be used to override or provide configuration properties at runtime, affecting how the application behaves.

This is correct. The spring.factories file is used by Spring Boot for loading auto-configuration classes and integrating third-party modules, directly impacting the application's configuration process.
prompt.explanation:
Spring Boot is designed to simplify application setup by providing sensible defaults and powerful configuration mechanisms. The framework determines its configuration by collecting input from multiple sources, allowing developers to fine-tune applications without extensive boilerplate.

1. External Configuration Files

Spring Boot automatically loads configuration from application.properties or application.yml files. These files can be placed in different locations (classpath, external directory, etc.), and their properties are used to set application behavior—like ports, database connections, logging, or feature flags. Properties can be overridden at different levels for flexibility.

2. Classpath Dependencies

Auto-configuration in Spring Boot is heavily dependent on the libraries present on the classpath. For example, if Spring Web is on the classpath, Spring Boot auto-configures web-related beans. Similarly, adding a database driver enables database-related auto-configuration. Removing or adding dependencies can drastically change the application's setup.

3. JVM Arguments

You can pass system properties to the JVM (e.g., -Dserver.port=9000) when starting the application. These arguments override settings from files and environment variables, providing a convenient way to change configuration per environment or deployment.

4. spring.factories Files

Spring Boot modules and third-party starters often use the spring.factories file to register auto-configuration classes and other extension points. This mechanism enables Spring Boot to automatically discover and apply configuration logic based on what’s available on the classpath.

Best Practices

  • Use external configuration files for most settings to keep configuration environment-specific and manageable.

  • Manage dependencies carefully to control what is auto-configured.

  • Use JVM arguments for quick overrides or secret management in deployment scripts.

  • Understand how spring.factories and auto-configuration work when creating your own Spring Boot starters or integrating third-party modules.

Example: Overriding the Server Port

java -jar myapp.jar --server.port=8081

or

# in application.properties
server.port=8081
answers: spring.factories file for different modules specified

results.question_plain: Which of the following factors influence the configuration setup performed by Spring Boot?

prompt.question: Which of the following methods is called first in the Spring bean lifecycle?
feedbacks: This method is not called first. It is invoked after dependency injection, but only after any method annotated with @PostConstruct is called.
This is also not called first. The init-method is executed after @PostConstruct and after afterPropertiesSet, if both are present.
This answer is correct. The @PostConstruct method is always called first after dependency injection and before afterPropertiesSet or any custom init-method. It is the earliest lifecycle callback.
Simply naming a method "init" does not make it part of the bean lifecycle unless it is specified in the XML configuration or annotated appropriately.
prompt.explanation:
Understanding the order of initialization callbacks in the Spring bean lifecycle is essential for writing reliable and maintainable beans. After Spring creates a bean instance and injects dependencies, it provides multiple hooks for initialization logic. The order in which these callbacks are invoked is important when you need to perform setup actions.

Order of Initialization Callbacks

  1. @PostConstruct
    Any method annotated with @PostConstruct is called immediately after dependency injection is complete, making it the first initialization callback. This is the recommended way to run initialization logic, as it is clear, concise, and works regardless of how the bean is defined (XML or annotations).

  2. afterPropertiesSet() (InitializingBean interface)
    If the bean implements the InitializingBean interface, the afterPropertiesSet() method is called after all @PostConstruct methods. This is an older style but still supported.

  3. Custom init-method (XML or @Bean configuration)
    If an init-method is specified in the bean's XML configuration (or via the @Bean(initMethod = "methodName") annotation), this method will be called last in the initialization phase, after the previous two callbacks.

Practical Example

@Component
public class MyBean implements InitializingBean {

    @PostConstruct
    public void postConstructInit() {
        System.out.println("PostConstruct called");
    }

    @Override
    public void afterPropertiesSet() {
        System.out.println("afterPropertiesSet called");
    }

    public void customInit() {
        System.out.println("Custom init-method called");
    }
}

In XML:

<bean id="myBean" class="com.example.MyBean" init-method="customInit"/>

The output order will be:

PostConstruct called
afterPropertiesSet called
Custom init-method called

Best Practices

  • Prefer @PostConstruct for most initialization logic as it is more modern and annotation-based.

  • Avoid combining multiple initialization mechanisms unless necessary, to keep the lifecycle simple and predictable.

  • Use init-method for legacy XML-based configuration or when you need to call a specific initialization method without changing the class.
answers: init-method as specified in the Spring XML Configuration

results.question_plain: Which of the following methods is called first in the Spring bean lifecycle?

prompt.question: Which HTTP verb is commonly used to create a new entity in REST APIs?
feedbacks: This verb is used to remove resources from the server, not to create new entities or modify existing ones.

Incorrect for this question. While PUT can be used to create a resource at a specific URI if it doesn't exist, it is primarily used for full replacement of existing resources. PUT is not typically the first choice for creating new entities in REST (POST is), though it can do so when the client specifies the URI.
This is a read-only operation used to retrieve data. It cannot create or modify resources.

This is the correct answer. POST is designed to create new entities, typically by submitting data to a collection resource. While POST is not idempotent and usually creates new resources, it is generally not used to modify existing ones directly.
prompt.explanation:
HTTP Methods for Resource Creation in REST APIs

When designing RESTful APIs with Spring, understanding the role of each HTTP method is crucial for building predictable and reliable interfaces. The most common methods are GET, POST, PUT, and DELETE, each serving a distinct purpose.

POST

The POST method is used to submit data to a resource, typically resulting in the creation of a new entity. When you send a POST request to an endpoint (for example, /api/users), the server processes the data and creates a new user entity. POST is not idempotent, meaning that sending the same request multiple times can result in multiple new entities being created.

Example in Spring:

@PostMapping("/users")
public User createUser(@RequestBody User user) {
    return userService.save(user);
}

PUT

The PUT method is intended for creating a new resource at a specified URI or for completely replacing an existing resource. PUT is idempotent: sending the same PUT request multiple times will always produce the same result. If the resource exists, it is replaced; if not, it is created.

Example in Spring:

@PutMapping("/users/{id}")
public User replaceUser(@PathVariable Long id, @RequestBody User user) {
    return userService.replace(id, user);
}

Other Verbs

  • GET is for retrieving data only—never for creation or modification.

  • DELETE removes resources and does not create or modify entities.

Best Practices

  • Use POST to create new entities when the server determines the URI (e.g., auto-generated IDs).

  • Use PUT when the client specifies the exact URI and for full resource replacement.

  • Never use GET or DELETE for creation or modification of resources.

results.question_plain: Which HTTP verb is commonly used to create a new entity in REST APIs?

prompt.question: Which HTTP verb is primarily used in REST to update an existing entity?
feedbacks: This is the correct answer. PUT is primarily used to update an existing entity or to create it at a specific URI if it does not exist. In RESTful APIs, PUT is idempotent, meaning repeated identical requests will always produce the same result.
This verb is used only to retrieve data from the server; it does not change or update any resources.
POST is typically used to create new resources, not to update existing ones. For partial updates, use PATCH; for full updates/replacement, use PUT.
This verb is used to remove an entity or resource from the server and is not intended for updates.
prompt.explanation:
In RESTful API design, the choice of HTTP verbs directly maps to the intended operation on resources. To update an existing entity, the PUT method is recommended and widely accepted as the standard.

How PUT Works

  • Updating an Entity:
    PUT is used to replace the entire resource at the target URI with the content provided in the request body. For example, sending a PUT request to /api/products/5 with a product representation will fully update or replace product with ID 5.

  • Idempotency:
    PUT is idempotent, which means that making the same request multiple times results in the same server state.

  • Creation (optional):
    If the specified resource does not exist, PUT can be used to create it at the given URI, though this behavior should be consistent with your API design.

Partial Updates

  • For partial updates (modifying only some fields of an entity), the PATCH verb is more appropriate. However, for full replacement or update, always use PUT.

Example in Spring

@PutMapping("/users/{id}")
public User updateUser(@PathVariable Long id, @RequestBody User user) {
    // update logic
    return userService.update(id, user);
}

Best Practices

  • Use PUT to fully replace resources.

  • Ensure the client sends a complete representation of the resource in the request body.

  • Always use PATCH for partial updates, not PUT.

  • Avoid using POST for updates to maintain REST semantics and clarity.

results.question_plain: Which HTTP verb is primarily used in REST to update an existing entity?

prompt.question: Which of the following are valid return types for a controller method annotated with @RequestMapping?
feedbacks: This is a valid return type. When a controller method returns void, the request is considered handled, and no explicit view name is returned; Spring infers the view name from the request path.

This is not a valid return type for a controller method in Spring MVC. Primitive types like double are not used as return types for controller methods, as Spring would not know how to map this directly to a view or response.
This is a valid and common return type. Returning a String usually represents the logical view name that Spring MVC should render, or it can be the response body if used with @ResponseBody.
Similar to double, this is not a typical or valid return type for controller methods in Spring MVC. It does not map to a view or represent a meaningful response.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring MVC, the return type of a controller method annotated with @RequestMapping determines how the framework processes the response. There are several options that allow for flexible handling of web requests:

Common Valid Return Types

  • String:
    Returning a String is the most common pattern in Spring MVC. The value is treated as the logical name of the view to be rendered (for example, a JSP page or a Thymeleaf template). If the method is annotated with @ResponseBody (or the controller is a @RestController), the returned String is sent directly as the HTTP response body.

    @RequestMapping("/hello")
public String sayHello() {
    return "hello"; // resolves to hello.jsp or hello.html
}

  • void:
    When a controller method returns void, no view name is explicitly returned. Instead, Spring infers the view name from the request path. This can be useful for simple cases or when the response is handled directly within the method (e.g., via the HttpServletResponse).

    @RequestMapping("/voidExample")
public void handleVoid(HttpServletResponse response) throws IOException {
    response.getWriter().write("Handled directly!");
}

Other Common Valid Return Types

  • ModelAndView: Allows returning both a model and a view name together.

  • Object/POJO: With @ResponseBody or in a @RestController, you can return Java objects, which will be serialized to JSON or XML.

  • ResponseEntity<T>: Used for full control over the response, including headers and status code.

results.question_plain: Which of the following are valid return types for a controller method annotated with @RequestMapping?

prompt.question: Which of the following are stereotype annotations?
feedbacks: This is a correct answer. @Repository is a stereotype annotation used to indicate that a class is a Data Access Object (DAO), allowing Spring to detect it for dependency injection and apply exception translation.
This is a correct answer. @Controller is a stereotype annotation that marks a class as a Spring MVC controller, allowing Spring to discover and register it for handling web requests.
This is a correct answer. @Service is a stereotype annotation used to define a service layer component, signaling Spring to treat the class as a business service and handle it as a Spring bean.

This is not a stereotype annotation. @Bean is a method-level annotation used in @Configuration classes to explicitly declare Spring beans, but it does not mark a class with a specific application role.
prompt.explanation:
Stereotype annotations in Spring are used to assign a specific role or meaning to a class within your application architecture. By using these annotations, you allow Spring to automatically detect, instantiate, and inject the appropriate beans into the application context. This helps to keep your codebase organized and maintainable.

Common Stereotype Annotations

  • @Component:
    The most generic stereotype annotation. It marks a class as a Spring component, making it eligible for auto-detection and dependency injection. Most other stereotype annotations are specializations of @Component.

  • @Repository:
    Marks a class as a Data Access Object (DAO). In addition to auto-detection, Spring applies exception translation to DAO methods, converting database-related exceptions into Spring's unified unchecked exceptions.

  • @Service:
    Indicates a service layer component, used for business logic. This annotation helps clearly distinguish service classes from other types of beans, improving code readability and intent.

  • @Controller:
    Used to mark a class as a web controller in a Spring MVC application. This makes the class eligible for handling HTTP requests, request mapping, and response rendering.

Example

@Repository
public class UserRepository { /* ... */ }

@Service
public class UserService { /* ... */ }

@Controller
public class UserController { /* ... */ }

Spring will automatically discover and manage these classes as beans based on their roles, thanks to the stereotype annotations.

Best Practices

  • Use stereotype annotations to clearly define the role of each class.

  • Favor @Component for generic components, and use @Repository, @Service, and @Controller for specific roles.

  • Use @Bean for manual bean registration, not for marking classes.

results.question_plain: Which of the following are stereotype annotations?

prompt.question: Beans of which type are required so you can work with JPA in Spring?
feedbacks: This is correct. To work with JPA in Spring, you need a transaction manager (specifically a JpaTransactionManager) to manage transactional boundaries for your entity operations.

This is correct. EntityManagerFactory is essential for JPA integration in Spring. It is responsible for creating EntityManager instances, which perform all persistence operations (CRUD). The EntityManagerFactory manages the entity metadata, persistence unit configuration, and provides the bridge between your JPA entities and the database via the DataSource. Without EntityManagerFactory, you cannot use JPA in Spring.
This is not required for JPA. JdbcTemplate is used for direct JDBC operations, not for ORM (Object-Relational Mapping) with JPA.
This is correct. A DataSource bean provides the connection pool for database access and is essential for JPA to connect to your database.
prompt.explanation:
When integrating JPA (Java Persistence API) with Spring, certain beans are essential for managing persistence and database connections. Spring Boot will typically auto-configure these for you, but understanding what is required helps you troubleshoot and customize your setup.

Key Required Beans

  • DataSource:
    The DataSource bean provides the connection pool for your application. JPA uses it to obtain connections to the underlying database. Spring Boot can auto-configure a DataSource if you include the relevant starter and set properties in application.properties or application.yml.

  • PlatformTransactionManager (specifically JpaTransactionManager):
    Spring requires a transaction manager to handle transactional boundaries for JPA entity operations. JpaTransactionManager is used for this purpose, enabling transactional annotations like @Transactional to work with your entity managers.

  • EntityManagerFactory:
    While not in the answers, it's worth mentioning: Spring creates an EntityManagerFactory for you based on your JPA configuration. This factory is responsible for creating EntityManager instances, which perform the actual persistence work.

What You Don’t Need

  • DriverManager:
    DriverManager is a standard JDBC class and not used directly by Spring or JPA in a bean context.

  • JdbcTemplate:
    JdbcTemplate is a utility for running raw JDBC queries, not for ORM. It’s not necessary if you’re only using JPA.

Example: Spring Boot Configuration

@SpringBootApplication
public class MyApplication {}

@Entity
public class User { /* ... */ }

@Repository
public interface UserRepository extends JpaRepository<User, Long> { }

With the right dependencies, Spring Boot will configure DataSource and JpaTransactionManager automatically.

Best Practices

  • Always ensure a DataSource is available in your context (by providing database configuration).

prompt.question: In a Spring test, which annotation can be used to mock a user?
feedbacks: This is not a valid annotation in Spring. There is no annotation named @MockAuthentication in Spring Test or Spring Security.

This is the correct answer. @WithMockUser is provided by Spring Security’s test support and allows you to simulate a user being authenticated in a test context, specifying roles, username, and authorities.
This annotation is from Mockito and is used to create mock objects for dependencies, not for mocking authenticated users.
This is a Spring Boot test annotation for replacing a bean with a Mockito mock in the application context. It is not used to mock user authentication.
prompt.explanation:
When writing tests for secured Spring applications, it is common to verify how controllers and service methods behave for different users and roles. Spring Security provides dedicated annotations and test utilities to simplify this process, enabling you to simulate (mock) authenticated users and control the security context for your tests.

The @WithMockUser Annotation

  • @WithMockUser is the standard way to mock an authenticated user for unit and integration tests in Spring Security.

  • You can specify attributes such as username, roles, and authorities to customize the simulated user.

  • It is typically used on test methods or class declarations.

Example Usage:

@Test
@WithMockUser(username = "admin", roles = {"ADMIN"})
public void testSecuredMethodAsAdmin() {
    // The security context will contain a user with username "admin" and role "ADMIN"
    // You can now test controller/service logic that requires authentication
}

How It Works

When @WithMockUser is present, Spring injects a mock Authentication object into the SecurityContext, bypassing the need for actual user details or a real login process. This allows you to focus on testing your application’s behavior rather than authentication mechanics.

Best Practices

  • Use @WithMockUser for fast, isolated tests where you only need to simulate an authenticated principal.

  • For more complex scenarios, use @WithUserDetails or custom SecurityContext setup.

  • Combine with @WebMvcTest or @SpringBootTest for complete controller/service testing with mocked security.

prompt.question: Which of the following statements about Spring Boot are true?
feedbacks: This statement is true. One of Spring Boot’s main goals is to simplify the process of creating stand-alone applications that can be run easily and are ready for production use.

This is also true. Spring Boot provides sensible defaults and configurations for both Spring and many popular third-party libraries, allowing developers to get started quickly without needing to make every decision themselves.
This is false. Spring Boot is designed to eliminate the need for XML configuration by favoring convention over configuration and leveraging annotation-based and properties-based configuration.
This is false. Spring Boot does not perform code generation; it configures and wires beans at runtime using auto-configuration, but it does not generate source code for your project.
prompt.explanation:
Spring Boot is a popular extension of the Spring framework that simplifies the setup and development of new Spring applications. Its main goal is to get you up and running quickly with minimal configuration and without the need for complex XML files or boilerplate code.

Key Features

  • Stand-Alone Applications:
    Spring Boot allows you to create applications that are self-contained and do not require external servers. You can run your application with a simple java -jar command, making deployment easy and predictable.

  • Opinionated Defaults:
    Spring Boot takes an "opinionated" view of how Spring projects should be structured, providing default configurations for common tasks (web servers, data access, monitoring, etc.). This drastically reduces the amount of manual configuration required.

  • No XML Configuration:
    Modern Spring Boot projects rarely require XML. Configuration is typically handled with annotations, Java config, or simple property files, making codebases easier to read and maintain.

  • No Code Generation:
    Unlike some frameworks, Spring Boot does not generate code or files. All configurations and beans are managed at runtime via Spring’s auto-configuration capabilities.

  • Production-Ready:
    Features like health checks, metrics, and externalized configuration are built in, making it easy to move from development to production.

Example: Running a Spring Boot App

With Spring Boot, the following single class is all you need to get started:

@SpringBootApplication
public class MyApplication {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SpringApplication.run(MyApplication.class, args);
    }
}

Best Practices

  • Prefer annotation-based and properties-based configuration over XML.

  • Leverage auto-configuration for rapid development and minimize manual setup.

  • Use Spring Boot starters to quickly add common dependencies and features.
answers: Spring Boot makes it easy to create stand-alone, production-grade Spring-based Applications.
Spring Boot takes an opinionated view of the Spring platform and third-party libraries
Spring Boot has many requirements for XML configuration.
Spring Boot does code generation using an opinionated approach based on the configurations done.

prompt.question: Which of these web-related types can be declared as parameters on a Spring MVC controller method and will be automatically provided by the framework?
feedbacks: This is correct. You can add a ServletRequest parameter (or its subinterface, such as HttpServletRequest) to a controller method, and Spring will automatically inject the current request object.

ApplicationContext is not a valid parameter type for a Spring MVC controller method. Spring only injects web-related context objects such as ServletRequest, ServletResponse, or HttpSession into handler parameters. The ApplicationContext is the global Spring container and, if needed, should be injected into the controller itself (e.g. via constructor or @Autowired), not passed as a method argument.
This is correct. Spring will automatically inject the current HTTP session (HttpSession) into your controller method if you declare it as a parameter.
This is correct. ServletResponse (or HttpServletResponse) can be declared as a parameter, and Spring will inject the current response object.
prompt.explanation:
Spring MVC lets you declare certain web-related types as parameters in your controller methods. For supported types, the framework automatically injects the corresponding servlet API objects, so you can work directly with the current HTTP request, response, or session.

Automatically injected web context types

ServletRequest / HttpServletRequest
Gives you access to the current HTTP request: headers, parameters, attributes, etc.

@GetMapping("/example")
public String handleRequest(ServletRequest request) {
    String param = request.getParameter("foo");
    return "view";
}

ServletResponse / HttpServletResponse
Allows you to work with the HTTP response: set status, headers, or write directly to the body.

@PostMapping("/submit")
public void handleResponse(ServletResponse response) throws IOException {
    response.getWriter().write("Hello, World!");
}

HttpSession
Represents the user’s HTTP session and lets you store and retrieve session attributes.

@GetMapping("/session")
public String handleSession(HttpSession session) {
    session.setAttribute("key", "value");
    return "sessionView";
}

All three – ServletRequest, ServletResponse, and HttpSession – are valid controller method parameters that Spring will supply automatically.

What is not injected as a method parameter

ApplicationContext is not injected as a controller method parameter.
It is the central Spring container managing all beans and configuration, not a per-request web object. If you need it, you obtain it via normal dependency injection (e.g. constructor injection or @Autowired), not as a handler method argument.

Best practice

Use these low-level servlet types only when you really need direct access to the HTTP layer. For most cases, prefer higher-level Spring MVC features like @RequestParam, @PathVariable, @RequestBody, Model, etc., which keep your controllers cleaner and easier to test.

prompt.question: Which of these are features of Spring AOP?
feedbacks: This is correct. Spring AOP can use CGLIB proxies to create proxies for classes that do not implement interfaces, enabling method interception and advice on such classes.
This is correct. Through the use of “introduction” advice (also known as inter-type declarations), Spring AOP can dynamically add new interfaces and implementations to proxied objects at runtime.
This is correct. If a target object does not implement any interface, Spring AOP uses CGLIB to create a subclass proxy, allowing advice to be applied even when no interfaces are present.
This is not correct for standard Spring AOP. Spring AOP only supports runtime weaving using proxies, not compile-time weaving. Compile-time weaving is a feature of AspectJ, not Spring AOP.
prompt.explanation:
Spring AOP (Aspect-Oriented Programming) provides a framework for modularizing cross-cutting concerns, such as logging, security, and transactions, without polluting business logic. It does so by dynamically creating proxies for beans and applying advice (such as interceptors) to method calls.

Key Features

  • Proxy-Based Architecture:
    Spring AOP uses proxies (JDK dynamic proxies or CGLIB proxies) to apply aspects to beans at runtime. If a bean implements at least one interface, Spring uses JDK dynamic proxies; otherwise, it uses CGLIB to generate a subclass proxy.

  • CGLIB Proxies:
    With CGLIB, Spring can proxy classes that do not implement interfaces. This extends AOP capabilities to a wider range of beans.

  • Interface Introduction:
    Spring AOP can introduce new interfaces and implementations to target objects at runtime using introduction advice. This lets you add new behaviors to existing beans dynamically.

  • Limitations:
    Standard Spring AOP works only at runtime and is limited to method interception. It does not support compile-time weaving or field-level interception, which are available in AspectJ (a more powerful AOP framework).

What Spring AOP Does Not Do

  • Compile-Time Weaving:
    Spring AOP cannot weave aspects at compile time. For compile-time or load-time weaving, you need AspectJ or other advanced AOP tools.

Example: Introduction Advice

public interface Auditable {
    void audit();
}

public class AuditableImpl implements Auditable {
    public void audit() {
        // Audit logic here
    }
}

// Using @DeclareParents in an aspect to introduce Auditable
@Aspect
public class AuditAspect {
    @DeclareParents(value = "com.example.*Service+", defaultImpl = AuditableImpl.class)
    public static Auditable auditable;
}

Best Practices

  • Use Spring AOP for typical cross-cutting concerns (logging, transactions, security) at the method level.

  • For advanced scenarios, such as compile-time weaving or field-level interception, consider AspectJ.

  • Keep in mind that Spring AOP is proxy-based and only affects beans managed by the Spring container.
answers: can introduce new interfaces (and a corresponding implementation) to an advised object
can proxy objects that does not implement an interface
can weave the aspects at compile time

results.question_plain: Which of these are features of Spring AOP?

prompt.question: Which of the following types can be returned by a method annotated with @RequestMapping in Spring MVC?
feedbacks: This is correct. Returning ModelAndView allows you to specify both the model data and the view name in one object, which Spring MVC will process to render the response.
This is incorrect. There is no Modal type in Spring MVC, but Model is typically used as a method parameter, not a return type.

This is correct. If a controller method returns void, Spring will infer the view name from the request path or the method can handle the response directly (for example, by writing to the HttpServletResponse).

This is correct. Returning a String typically represents the logical view name for rendering. In some cases (with @ResponseBody or in a @RestController), the returned string may be sent as the HTTP response body.
prompt.explanation:
Spring MVC provides flexibility in how controller methods return data to the framework for rendering web responses. The return type determines how Spring processes the result and what is sent back to the client.

Common Valid Return Types

  • String:
    Returning a String typically specifies the name of the view to render. For example, returning "home" tells Spring to render the "home" view template. If the method or controller is annotated with @ResponseBody (or it's a @RestController), the returned string is sent as the HTTP response body.

    @RequestMapping("/hello")
public String sayHello() {
    return "helloView"; // resolves to helloView.jsp or helloView.html
}

  • ModelAndView:
    This type allows you to bundle the model data and the view name together. It’s especially useful when you want to add attributes to the model and specify the view in one object.

    @RequestMapping("/user")
public ModelAndView user() {
    ModelAndView mav = new ModelAndView("userView");
    mav.addObject("username", "john");
    return mav;
}

  • Void:
    When a method returns void, Spring infers the view name from the request path or you handle the response directly (e.g., by writing to the HttpServletResponse).

    @RequestMapping("/plain")
public void handleDirect(HttpServletResponse response) throws IOException {
    response.getWriter().write("Handled directly!");
}

Invalid Return Types

  • Modal:
    There is no such type in Spring MVC. Model can be used as a method parameter to add attributes, but it is not a valid return type.

Other Valid Return Types

Other valid types include domain objects (for use with @ResponseBody), ResponseEntity<T> (for advanced HTTP response control), and collections of domain objects for REST APIs.

Best Practices

  • Use String for simple view name resolution.

  • Use ModelAndView when you need to pass both model data and a view.

  • Use void only when handling the response directly or relying on default view name inference.

  • For REST APIs, return domain objects or ResponseEntity<T> with @ResponseBody.

prompt.question: What is the correct format for specifying tags as URL parameters when accessing specific metrics in the metrics endpoint?
feedbacks: This is incorrect. This format does not match how tags are specified in Spring Boot’s metrics endpoint URL parameters.
This is incorrect. The format with two equal signs is not valid for tagging in the metrics endpoint.
This is incorrect. Using a question mark in the tag format is not the standard for URL parameters in this context.

This is correct. The correct format for specifying tags as URL parameters when querying the /actuator/metrics endpoint in Spring Boot is tag=KEY:VALUE. This allows you to filter the metrics by a specific tag key and value.
prompt.explanation:
Spring Boot Actuator provides production-ready features for monitoring and managing applications, including the /actuator/metrics endpoint. This endpoint allows you to retrieve detailed metrics about your application, such as JVM stats, HTTP request counts, and custom metrics.

Filtering by Tags

Many metrics (like HTTP requests, database connection pools, or custom counters) are tagged with additional information, such as status codes, URIs, or method names. When querying the /actuator/metrics/{metricName} endpoint, you can use URL parameters to filter results by tag.

Correct URL Parameter Format

  • The format to filter by a tag is:
    tag=KEY:VALUE

  • You can specify multiple tags by repeating the parameter:

This format allows you to zero in on the exact metric values you care about, such as requests to a specific endpoint or responses with a specific status code.

Example

If you want to get metrics for HTTP server requests to /api/users with status 200:

GET /actuator/metrics/http.server.requests?tag=uri:/api/users&tag=status:200

Best Practices

  • Use multiple tag parameters for multiple filters.

prompt.question: Which of the following statements regarding the @Query annotation are correct?
feedbacks: This is correct. The @Query annotation, when used in Spring Data JPA repositories, parses JPQL (Java Persistence Query Language) by default unless the nativeQuery attribute is set to true.
This is incorrect. Native SQL will only be parsed if you set nativeQuery = true in the annotation; otherwise, JPQL is assumed.
This is correct. The @Query annotation can only be applied to methods (not classes or fields) in repository interfaces to define custom queries.
This is correct. The @Query annotation supports Spring Expression Language (SpEL) in certain contexts, especially for dynamic projections and query parameters.
prompt.explanation:
The @Query annotation in Spring Data JPA is a powerful tool for defining custom queries directly on repository interface methods. It helps you write JPQL or SQL queries inline, rather than relying solely on method name conventions.

Key Features

  • Default to JPQL:
    By default, the query defined in @Query is interpreted as JPQL (Java Persistence Query Language). This language operates on entity objects, not directly on database tables.

    @Query("SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.email = ?1")
User findByEmail(String email);

  • Native Queries:
    If you need to use raw SQL, you can specify nativeQuery = true:

    @Query(value = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = ?1", nativeQuery = true)
User findByEmailNative(String email)

  • Method-Level Only:
    @Query is strictly a method-level annotation and cannot be applied to classes or fields. It's used directly on repository methods to provide custom query logic.

  • Supports SpEL:
    The annotation supports SpEL (Spring Expression Language) for advanced scenarios, such as dynamic query parameters or projections. For example:

    @Query("SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.id = :#{#user.id}")
User findByUser(@Param("user") User user);

Best Practices

  • Use JPQL for most queries as it is portable across different JPA providers.

  • Switch to native SQL only for performance-critical or database-specific operations.

  • Leverage SpEL for dynamic and flexible query parameters.

  • Always keep @Query annotations on repository methods for clarity and maintainability.
answers: By default it will parse JPQL
By default it will parse native SQL

prompt.question: Which of the following annotations are used as meta-annotations for @SpringBootApplication in the Spring Boot framework?
feedbacks: This is correct. @SpringBootConfiguration is a specialized form of @Configuration and is used as a meta-annotation within @SpringBootApplication to indicate that the class provides Spring Boot application configuration.
This is correct. @EnableAutoConfiguration enables Spring Boot’s auto-configuration mechanism and is directly included as a meta-annotation in @SpringBootApplication.
This is incorrect. @Controller is a stereotype annotation for Spring MVC controllers and is not included as a meta-annotation in @SpringBootApplication.

This is correct. @ComponentScan is included as a meta-annotation in @SpringBootApplication. It enables component scanning, allowing Spring to discover and register beans in the package of the main class and all its sub-packages.
prompt.explanation:
The @SpringBootApplication annotation is a convenient shortcut introduced in Spring Boot to simplify the main application class configuration. It combines several important Spring Boot annotations into a single, easy-to-use annotation, making your configuration cleaner and more concise.

What Does @SpringBootApplication Include?

When you use @SpringBootApplication, you are actually using a combination of three core annotations:

  • @SpringBootConfiguration:
    This annotation is itself a specialization of @Configuration, indicating that the class is a source of bean definitions for the application context. It tells Spring Boot that this class can be used for Java-based configuration.

  • @EnableAutoConfiguration:
    This powerful annotation tells Spring Boot to automatically configure your application based on the dependencies present on the classpath. It saves you from needing to write a lot of boilerplate configuration code.

  • @ComponentScan:
    This annotation instructs Spring to scan the package of the annotated class (and its sub-packages) for components, configurations, and services to automatically register as beans. While @Component is used for marking beans, @ComponentScan is the annotation that gets included as a meta-annotation.

Example

@SpringBootApplication
public class MyApplication {
    public static void main(String[] args) {
        SpringApplication.run(MyApplication.class, args);
    }
}

This single annotation activates all the underlying mechanisms needed for most Spring Boot projects.

Best Practices

  • Always annotate your main application class with @SpringBootApplication for new Spring Boot projects.

  • Understand that this single annotation brings in multiple configuration mechanisms, including auto-configuration and component scanning.

  • Only override these defaults (e.g., by manually using @ComponentScan or @EnableAutoConfiguration) if you have advanced requirements.

prompt.question:
Is the following statement correct?

In Spring MVC the Front Controller design pattern is implemented by the usage of DispatcherServlet
links: [A Guide to the Front Controller Pattern in Java](https://www.baeldung.com/java-front-controller-pattern)
feedbacks: This is correct. In Spring MVC, the Front Controller design pattern is implemented through the DispatcherServlet, which acts as a single entry point for handling all incoming HTTP requests and dispatching them to appropriate controllers.
This is incorrect. The DispatcherServlet in Spring MVC is precisely the implementation of the Front Controller design pattern.
prompt.explanation:
The Front Controller design pattern is a classic web architecture pattern in which a single central controller handles and routes all requests coming into an application. This centralization enables consistent request handling, logging, security, and other cross-cutting concerns before dispatching to application-specific handlers.

DispatcherServlet in Spring MVC

In Spring MVC, the DispatcherServlet is the front controller. It is defined in the web application's configuration and mapped to one or more URL patterns (typically /). When an HTTP request comes in, the DispatcherServlet:

  1. Receives the request.

  2. Identifies the appropriate controller (handler) for the URL and HTTP method.

  3. Delegates processing to that controller.

  4. Coordinates with other components (such as view resolvers, exception resolvers, and handler interceptors) to generate the response.

This pattern provides a flexible and maintainable structure, ensuring that cross-cutting concerns can be handled in one place, and that request handling logic is decoupled from application logic.

Example Configuration

With Spring Boot, the DispatcherServlet is auto-configured and mapped for you. In a traditional web.xml, you might see:

<servlet>
    <servlet-name>dispatcher</servlet-name>
    <servlet-class>org.springframework.web.servlet.DispatcherServlet</servlet-class>
</servlet>
<servlet-mapping>
    <servlet-name>dispatcher</servlet-name>
    <url-pattern>/</url-pattern>
</servlet-mapping>

Best Practices

  • Let the DispatcherServlet handle all incoming requests by mapping it to /.

  • Use controllers, handler methods, and other Spring MVC features for routing and response generation.

  • Leverage the centralized nature of the front controller for logging, authentication, and error handling.

results.question_plain: Is the following statement correct? In Spring MVC the Front Controller design pattern is implemented by the usage of DispatcherServlet

prompt.question: Which of the following statements are correct, regarding @Value?

This is incorrect. @Value can be used not only on fields, but also on method parameters, including constructor parameters, making it very flexible for value injection.

This is partially correct, but not entirely accurate. While @Value can inject values similarly to @Inject or @Autowired, @Value is specifically for injecting primitive values, property values, or SpEL results, not beans. @Inject is used for dependency injection of beans, not for simple values.
This is correct. One of the primary uses of @Value is to inject property values, strings, numbers, or other simple types from configuration files or environment variables.
prompt.explanation:
The @Value annotation in Spring is a versatile tool for injecting simple values into your beans. It allows you to pull configuration properties, literals, or results of expressions directly into fields, method parameters, or constructor parameters. This helps decouple hard-coded values from your source code and supports dynamic, environment-driven configuration.

Key Features of @Value

  • Supports SpEL (Spring Expression Language):
    You can use SpEL expressions within the @Value annotation to compute values at runtime. For example, you can reference other beans, perform calculations, or apply string operations.

    @Value("#{2 * 5}")
private int result; // Injects 10

  • Injects Property and Environment Values:
    You can inject values from application.properties, environment variables, or system properties. This is commonly used to externalize configuration.

    @Value("${app.title}")

  • Flexible Usage:
    @Value can be placed on fields, setter methods, method parameters, or constructor parameters.

    @Component
public class MyBean {
    private final String name;
    public MyBean(@Value("${my.name}") String name) {
        this.name = name;
    }
}

  • Injects Simple Types:
    Use @Value to inject strings, numbers, booleans, lists, or any simple type that can be converted from a string.

Best Practices

  • Use @Value for injecting configuration or literal values, not for injecting other beans.

  • Prefer @ConfigurationProperties for complex or grouped property bindings.

  • Avoid hardcoding values directly in code—externalize them and inject with @Value or property binding.

@Value can be used to inject simple values like Strings, number, etc.

prompt.question: In which scenarios should a transaction be declared as readOnly in a Spring application to optimize performance and ensure data integrity?
feedbacks: This is correct. Declaring a transaction as readOnly when performing large read operations can improve performance by allowing the database and transaction manager to optimize for read-only access.
This is correct. Use readOnly when you want to ensure the transaction does not modify data, helping enforce data integrity and avoid unintended updates.
This is correct. If the transaction only involves reading data and never performs any writes, marking it as readOnly is both safe and optimal.
This is incorrect. The save method writes data to the database, so the transaction should not be declared as readOnly in this scenario.
prompt.explanation:
Marking a transaction as readOnly in Spring applications is an important optimization that can boost performance and protect your data. The readOnly flag tells the underlying database and transaction manager that no data changes will occur within the transaction. This enables optimizations at several levels:

Benefits of readOnly Transactions

  • Performance Optimization:
    Databases may skip locking mechanisms, avoid unnecessary change tracking, or leverage specialized caches for read-only operations, which can improve performance, especially when dealing with large result sets or high-concurrency environments.

  • Data Integrity:
    By declaring a transaction as readOnly, you help prevent accidental data modifications during queries that are only supposed to read data. Spring may even throw exceptions if you attempt to write in a read-only transaction (depending on the database and configuration).

  • Clear Intent:
    Using readOnly signals to other developers and maintainers that the transaction is strictly for data retrieval, improving code clarity and maintainability.

When to Use readOnly = true

  • When performing large or complex read operations (e.g., reporting, analytics, batch data exports).

  • When you are certain no write (insert/update/delete) operations are performed within the transaction.

  • When you want to enforce data integrity by protecting against unintended writes.

When NOT to Use readOnly = true

  • Whenever you are creating, updating, or deleting entities (such as using repository save, delete, etc.).

  • In mixed transactions where both reads and writes occur.

Example Usage

@Transactional(readOnly = true)
public List<User> findAllUsers() {
    return userRepository.findAll();
}

Best Practices

  • Always set readOnly = true for service or repository methods that only fetch data.

  • Never use readOnly when performing any data modification.

  • Leverage this flag in performance-critical areas, especially for high-volume read operations.
answers: when a large set of data is read to optimize performance
when no changes should be made to the database
when it does not include any write operations
when save method from repository is performed

results.question_plain: In which scenarios should a transaction be declared as readOnly in a Spring application to optimize performance and ensure data integrity?

prompt.question: Which of the following statements about SecurityFilterChain in Spring Security are correct?
feedbacks: This is correct. In Spring Security, you can configure multiple SecurityFilterChain beans, each mapped to specific URL patterns to apply different security rules for different parts of your application.

This is incorrect. SecurityFilterChain contains a list of security filters, not a list of FilterChainProxy instances. There is usually one FilterChainProxy per application, which holds the filter chains.
This is correct. A SecurityFilterChain is essentially an ordered list of security filters that are applied to requests matching its URL pattern.
This is incorrect. DelegatingFilterProxy delegates calls to a Spring bean of type Filter (commonly a FilterChainProxy), not directly to a SecurityFilterChain.
prompt.explanation:
Spring Security uses a flexible filter-based architecture for handling security in web applications. The key building block for configuring HTTP security is the SecurityFilterChain.

How SecurityFilterChain Works

  • URL Pattern Matching:
    Each SecurityFilterChain is linked to specific URL patterns. This allows you to apply different security configurations to different endpoints within the same application (for example, public vs. admin APIs).

    @Bean
public SecurityFilterChain apiFilterChain(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
    http
      .securityMatcher("/api/**")
      .authorizeHttpRequests(authz -> authz.anyRequest().authenticated());
    return http.build();
}

  • List of Security Filters:
    A SecurityFilterChain is fundamentally a sequence (list) of security filters (such as UsernamePasswordAuthenticationFilter, CsrfFilter, etc.). Incoming requests that match the chain's URL pattern are processed by these filters in order.

  • FilterChainProxy and DelegatingFilterProxy:
    There is typically one FilterChainProxy bean that holds all the configured SecurityFilterChain instances. The servlet container’s filter mechanism calls a DelegatingFilterProxy, which then delegates to the FilterChainProxy.

  • Notable Misconceptions:

    • The DelegatingFilterProxy delegates to a Filter bean (usually FilterChainProxy), not directly to a SecurityFilterChain.

    • SecurityFilterChain does not contain other FilterChainProxy instances; it only contains filters.

Best Practices

  • Use multiple SecurityFilterChain beans for fine-grained, URL-specific security configuration.

  • Understand the flow: DelegatingFilterProxy → FilterChainProxy → SecurityFilterChain → [list of filters].
answers: Each instance of SecurityFilterChain is associated with a specific URL pattern.
Each instance of SecurityFilterChain contains a list of FilterChainProxy instances.

prompt.question: In a Spring Boot web application, which embedded server is auto-configured by default?
feedbacks: This is incorrect. Jetty is an alternative embedded server that can be used in Spring Boot, but it is not the default. To use Jetty, you must exclude Tomcat and add the spring-boot-starter-jetty dependency.

This is correct. Apache Tomcat is the default embedded servlet container for Spring Boot web applications when using spring-boot-starter-web. It is automatically configured and allows you to run your application with a simple java -jar command.

This is incorrect. Undertow is another alternative embedded server that can be used with Spring Boot, but it is not the default. To use Undertow, you must exclude Tomcat and add the spring-boot-starter-undertow dependency.

This is incorrect. Netty is used as the default server for reactive applications with Spring WebFlux (spring-boot-starter-webflux), not for traditional Spring MVC applications. For Spring MVC (spring-boot-starter-web), Tomcat is the default.
prompt.explanation:
Spring Boot simplifies web application development by auto-configuring an embedded servlet container, so you don't have to deploy your app to an external server. This embedded server is what allows you to run your Spring Boot application with just a simple java -jar command.

Which Server Is Used by Default?

  • Apache Tomcat:
    For traditional Spring Boot web applications (using Spring MVC), Apache Tomcat is the default embedded server. It's included automatically when you use the spring-boot-starter-web dependency.

  • Alternative Servers:
    If you prefer, you can easily switch to Jetty or Undertow by excluding Tomcat and adding the desired starter (spring-boot-starter-jetty or spring-boot-starter-undertow) to your project dependencies.

  • Netty:
    Netty is only used by default if you are building a reactive application with the spring-boot-starter-webflux dependency.

Example: Default Setup

Your pom.xml (Maven) or build.gradle (Gradle) will include:

<dependency>
    <groupId>org.springframework.boot</groupId>
    <artifactId>spring-boot-starter-web</artifactId>
</dependency>

This will auto-configure Tomcat as your embedded servlet container.

Best Practices

  • Use the default Tomcat server for most Spring Boot web applications, unless you have a specific requirement for Jetty or Undertow.

  • If you need to change the embedded server, update your dependencies accordingly and exclude Tomcat.

  • Take advantage of embedded server features to simplify your deployment and testing.

To change the location, you must use spring.config.location

How to Change the Location

prompt.question: At which of the following levels can Spring Security be implemented to enforce access control?
feedbacks: This is correct. Spring Security can enforce access control at the class level using annotations like @PreAuthorize, @Secured, or by applying security rules to all methods within a class.

This is correct. Method-level security is a core feature in Spring Security, allowing you to apply access rules to individual methods with annotations such as @PreAuthorize, @PostAuthorize, @Secured, or @RolesAllowed.
This is incorrect. Spring Security does not provide field-level access control; it does not restrict access to individual fields of a class.

This is correct. Web (URL) level security is fundamental in Spring Security, allowing you to define which HTTP endpoints (URLs) require authentication or specific roles using HTTP security configuration.
prompt.explanation:
Spring Security offers a comprehensive framework for applying access control at multiple levels within your application. Understanding where and how to enforce security ensures your application is both robust and flexible.

1. Class-Level Security

You can restrict access to all methods within a class by annotating the class with security annotations like @Secured, @PreAuthorize, or @RolesAllowed. This makes all methods in the class subject to the same access rules, reducing repetitive annotations.

@Secured("ROLE_ADMIN")
public class AdminService {
    // All methods require ADMIN role
}

2. Method-Level Security

Spring Security can enforce access at the method level, allowing granular control over who can invoke specific business logic.

@PreAuthorize("hasRole('USER')")
public void userAction() { ... }

You enable this feature by adding @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity(prePostEnabled = true) to your configuration.

3. Web-Level (URL-Level) Security

Web security is the most common use case, where you define access rules for URLs and HTTP endpoints via HttpSecurity configuration.

@Override
protected void configure(HttpSecurity http) throws Exception {
    http.authorizeRequests()
        .antMatchers("/admin/**").hasRole("ADMIN")
        .anyRequest().authenticated();
}

4. Field-Level Security

Spring Security does not natively support field-level access control. If you need to secure specific data fields, you must implement custom logic or use additional libraries, as this is outside standard Spring Security functionality.

Best Practices

  • Use method-level or class-level security for business logic and service methods.

  • Always combine web-level (URL) security with method-level security for defense in depth.

  • Do not rely on field-level restrictions for sensitive data—design APIs to only expose necessary data.

results.question_plain: At which of the following levels can Spring Security be implemented to enforce access control?
