feedbacks: The bean ID is determined by the method name annotated with @Bean, which in this case is "prodDataSource". Therefore, the created bean's ID will be "prodDataSource".

The bean ID is not based on the variable name within the method, but rather on the method name itself. In this case, the bean ID will not be "driverManagerDataSource" as it is not the method name annotated with @Bean.

The bean ID is not based on the variable name within the method, but rather on the method name itself. In this case, the bean ID will not be "dataSource" as it is not the method name annotated with @Bean.
The bean ID is determined by the method name annotated with @Bean, which in this case is "prodDataSource". The class name "DataSourceConfig" is not used as the bean ID in this scenario.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring, when defining beans using the @Bean annotation inside a @Configuration class, the bean ID (or bean name) is determined by default from the method name that declares the bean.

For example:

@Configuration
public class DataSourceConfig {

    @Bean
    public DataSource prodDataSource() {
        // create and configure a DataSource
        return new DriverManagerDataSource();
    }
}

  • The method is named prodDataSource.

  • Since @Bean has no explicit name or value attribute specified, the bean’s ID defaults to the method name.

  • Therefore, the Spring container will register this bean with the ID prodDataSource.

If you wanted to override this default behavior, you could explicitly set a name:

@Bean(name = "myDataSource")
public DataSource prodDataSource() {
    return new DriverManagerDataSource();
}

In that case, the bean ID would be myDataSource instead of prodDataSource.

Other identifiers inside the method (like dataSource variable name or class name DataSourceConfig) do not determine the bean ID.

prompt.question: Which of the following statements correctly describes how @Transactional behaves by default in Spring?
feedbacks:
By default, Spring's @Transactional only rolls back for unchecked exceptions — that is, RuntimeException and Error.
If you want Spring to roll back on checked exceptions, you must explicitly specify this with rollbackFor.

@Transactional(rollbackFor = IOException.class)
This is the opposite of default behavior. Spring rolls back the transaction if a RuntimeException or Error is thrown.

This is the default behavior in Spring. If a method annotated with @Transactional throws a RuntimeException or Error, Spring will roll back the transaction.


Checked exceptions like IOException or SQLException do not trigger rollback by default.

Spring does not always suspend the current transaction. That behavior depends on the propagation setting (REQUIRED, REQUIRES_NEW, etc.).


The default is Propagation.REQUIRED, meaning it will join the existing transaction if one exists, or create a new one if not.
prompt.explanation:
The @Transactional annotation in Spring is a powerful mechanism that simplifies transaction management in enterprise applications. It allows you to declaratively manage transaction boundaries without dealing with boilerplate code. Here's what every Spring developer should understand about its default behavior:

1. What Is @Transactional?

@Transactional is an annotation provided by Spring to declaratively demarcate transaction boundaries. When applied to a method or class, it ensures that the method runs within a transactional context. If any failure occurs that meets rollback criteria, the entire transaction is rolled back automatically.

2. Rollback Rules – Default Behavior

By default, Spring's transaction management rolls back transactions only for unchecked exceptions, which means:

  • Rollback will occur if the method throws:

    • Any subclass of RuntimeException (e.g. IllegalArgumentException, NullPointerException)

    • Any subclass of Error (e.g. OutOfMemoryError, StackOverflowError)

  • Rollback will NOT occur if the method throws:

    • Checked exceptions like IOException, SQLException, or custom checked exceptions

This default behavior is designed to prevent rollbacks on common recoverable exceptions unless explicitly requested. If you need to roll back on a checked exception, you must specify it explicitly using the rollbackFor attribute:

@Transactional(rollbackFor = IOException.class)
public void processFile() throws IOException {
    // Will trigger rollback on IOException
}

Similarly, if you want to avoid rollback for a specific exception, you can use noRollbackFor:

@Transactional(noRollbackFor = IllegalArgumentException.class)
public void doSomething() {
    throw new IllegalArgumentException("Will not cause rollback");
}

3. Transaction Propagation – Default Behavior

The default propagation setting for @Transactional is REQUIRED.

This means:

  • If a transaction is already in progress, the method joins that existing transaction.

  • If no transaction is active, a new one is created.

This behavior ensures seamless nesting of transactional methods. However, if you need to always run a method in a new transaction, regardless of the current context, you can use:

@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.REQUIRES_NEW)
public void independentOperation() {
    // Always runs in a new transaction
}

There are several other propagation levels, such as MANDATORY, SUPPORTS, NESTED, each with specific behavior depending on transaction presence. These are useful in complex transactional flows.

4. Where Should @Transactional Be Applied?

@Transactional is typically applied to methods in the service layer. This layer is responsible for coordinating business logic and database operations, making it the ideal place for transaction boundaries.

You can annotate either the method or the entire class:

@Service
@Transactional
public class OrderService {
    public void createOrder() {
        // This method runs within a transaction
    }
}

If applied at the class level, all public methods of the class will be transactional. Private methods are never affected by @Transactional.

5. Self-Invocation Limitation

A very important nuance is that method calls within the same class bypass transactional proxies. This means if a transactional method is called from another method in the same class, it won’t go through the Spring proxy, and the transactional behavior won’t be applied.

Incorrect:

@Transactional
public void methodA() {
    methodB(); // This does NOT go through the proxy!
}

@Transactional
public void methodB() {
    // Will not be transactional
}

Correct workaround: Retrieve the proxy from the Spring context:

@Autowired
private ApplicationContext context;

public void methodA() {
    context.getBean(getClass()).methodB(); // Goes through proxy, so transactional
}

6. Read-Only Transactions

For methods that only read data (without modifying it), you can optimize performance using:

@Transactional(readOnly = true)
public List<User> findAllUsers() {
    return userRepository.findAll();
}

This hints to the underlying transaction manager that the method will not perform updates, allowing certain optimizations at the database or persistence provider level (e.g. disabling dirty tracking in Hibernate).

7. Nested Transactions

Spring supports nested transactions using Propagation.NESTED. This is useful when you want a sub-operation to run in its own transaction, but still be part of a parent transaction’s scope.

@Transactional
public void parentMethod() {
    childMethod(); // Runs in a nested transaction
}

@Transactional(propagation = Propagation.NESTED)
public void childMethod() {
    // Can rollback independently
}

Be aware that nested transactions require support from the underlying transaction manager, such as JDBC savepoints.

8. Transaction Manager Configuration

In most Spring Boot applications, the transaction manager is automatically configured based on the database used (e.g. DataSourceTransactionManager for JDBC, JpaTransactionManager for JPA/Hibernate).

You can still define it explicitly if needed:

@Bean
public PlatformTransactionManager transactionManager(EntityManagerFactory emf) {
    return new JpaTransactionManager(emf);
}

Conclusion

Understanding how @Transactional behaves by default in Spring is crucial for ensuring data integrity, performance, and predictable error handling. It allows you to declaratively define transaction boundaries without cluttering your business logic with boilerplate code. By mastering its configuration and limitations, you'll write safer, more reliable applications that scale cleanly.
answers: It rolls back the transaction for both checked and unchecked exceptions by default.
It commits the transaction even if a RuntimeException is thrown.
It rolls back the transaction only for unchecked exceptions (subclasses of RuntimeException and Error).
It always suspends the current transaction and starts a new one.

results.question_plain: Which of the following statements correctly describes how @Transactional behaves by default in Spring?

prompt.question: Which of the following methods can be used to handle dependencies between beans in Spring?
feedbacks: Using the @Inject annotation on the dependent bean's method allows for dependency injection in Spring. This annotation can be used to inject dependencies into the method parameters, providing a way to handle dependencies between beans.

The depends-on attribute in the bean configuration is used to specify the beans that a particular bean depends on. By using this attribute, you can define the order in which beans should be initialized, allowing for the handling of dependencies between beans in Spring.

The @Required annotation is used to indicate that a particular bean property must be set during configuration. While this annotation can ensure that a required dependency is present, it is not specifically designed for handling dependencies between beans in Spring.

Using the @Autowired annotation on the dependent bean's constructor allows for automatic dependency injection in Spring. By annotating the constructor with @Autowired, Spring will automatically inject the required dependencies, providing a way to handle dependencies between beans effectively.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring, Dependency Injection (DI) is the primary mechanism for handling dependencies between beans. The Spring IoC container automatically wires beans together, reducing manual configuration and promoting loose coupling. There are several ways to manage these dependencies:

  1. @Autowired

    • The most commonly used annotation in Spring for dependency injection.

    • Can be applied to constructors, setters, or fields.

    • Constructor injection with @Autowired is the most recommended approach, as it enforces immutability and makes dependencies explicit.

    • Example:

      @Component
public class OrderService {
    private final PaymentService paymentService;

    @Autowired
    public OrderService(PaymentService paymentService) {
        this.paymentService = paymentService;
    }
}

  2. @Inject (JSR-330 standard)

    • Works similarly to @Autowired, but is part of the JSR-330 standard, making code less Spring-specific.

    • Example:

      @Inject
private PaymentService paymentService;

  3. depends-on attribute

    • Declared in XML or Java configuration to enforce initialization order between beans.

    • Useful when a bean must be created before another (e.g., caching service before dependent components).

    • Example (XML):

      <bean id="orderService" class="com.example.OrderService" depends-on="paymentService"/>

  4. @Required (legacy and discouraged)

    • Used on setter methods to enforce that a property must be set during configuration.

    • Not recommended in modern Spring applications, since constructor injection achieves the same goal more cleanly.
answers: Use the @Inject annotation on the dependent bean's method.
Use the depends-on attribute in the bean configuration.
Use the @Required annotation on the dependent bean's field.
Use the @Autowired annotation on the dependent bean's constructor.

results.question_plain: Which of the following methods can be used to handle dependencies between beans in Spring?

prompt.question: Which of the following statements about the usage of the @Bean annotation in Spring are correct?
feedbacks: @Bean is not a specification of @Component in Spring. While both annotations are used for bean creation and management, they serve different purposes and have different functionalities within the Spring framework.

Using @Bean on top of a method outside a @Configuration-annotated class will not result in a compilation error in Spring. The @Bean annotation can be used on methods in regular Spring-managed components to define and register beans.

The @Bean annotation can indeed be used on top of a method inside a @Configuration-annotated class to register a bean in the Spring application context. This is a common practice in Spring configuration classes to define and configure beans.

@Bean is typically used on methods within a @Configuration-annotated class to declare and define beans in Spring. It is not commonly used on fields inside a configuration class to declare dependencies, as beans are usually created and configured through methods.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring, the @Bean annotation is used to explicitly declare a bean definition within the application context. It is most commonly applied to methods inside a @Configuration class, allowing developers to create and configure beans in Java code rather than using XML.

Key points about @Bean:

  1. @Bean vs @Component

    • @Bean is not a specialization of @Component.

    • @Component is a class-level stereotype annotation that marks a class as a candidate for component scanning.

    • @Bean is a method-level annotation that tells Spring to register the method’s return value as a bean.

  2. Location of @Bean methods

    • The most common and recommended place is inside a class annotated with @Configuration.

    • Example:

      @Configuration
public class AppConfig {
    @Bean
    public MyService myService() {
        return new MyServiceImpl();
    }
}

    • It can also be placed inside classes annotated with @Component, @Service, etc., but then it won’t benefit from full CGLIB proxying behavior that @Configuration provides (like ensuring singleton semantics when a @Bean method calls another @Bean method).

  3. Compilation behavior

    • Declaring @Bean outside of @Configuration will not cause a compilation error. Instead, it may limit functionality, and Spring treats it more like a direct bean factory method.

  4. Incorrect usage

    • @Bean is not used on fields. Dependencies are typically injected into fields using @Autowired, @Inject, or constructor injection.

    • Putting @Bean on a field does nothing meaningful and is not valid practice.
answers: Using @Bean on top of a method outside a @Configuration-annotated class will result in a compilation error
The @Bean annotation can be used on top of a method inside a @Configuration-annotated class to register a bean
@Bean can be used on fields inside a @Configuration-annotated class to declare a dependency

results.question_plain: Which of the following statements about the usage of the @Bean annotation in Spring are correct?

prompt.question: Which of the following methods is used for customizing the destruction phase of a bean's lifecycle and is part of the DisposableBean interface in Spring?
feedbacks: The postConstruct() method is used for customizing the initialization phase of a bean's lifecycle in Spring, not the destruction phase. It is typically annotated with @PostConstruct and is called after the bean has been constructed and all dependencies have been injected.

The afterPropertiesSet() method is part of the InitializingBean interface in Spring and is used for customizing the initialization phase of a bean's lifecycle, not the destruction phase. It is called after all properties have been set on the bean.

The destroy() method is used for customizing the destruction phase of a bean's lifecycle in Spring. It is part of the DisposableBean interface and is called when the Spring container is shutting down, allowing the bean to release any resources it has acquired during its lifecycle.

The onInit() method is not a standard method in the Spring framework for customizing the lifecycle of a bean. It is not part of any interface like DisposableBean or InitializingBean, and therefore is not used for defining the destruction phase of a bean's lifecycle.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring, beans go through a lifecycle that consists of several phases: instantiation, dependency injection, initialization, and finally destruction. Developers can hook into these lifecycle phases to perform custom logic, such as resource allocation or cleanup.

For the destruction phase, Spring provides multiple mechanisms:

  1. DisposableBean interface

    • Defines a single method:

      void destroy() throws Exception;

    • If a bean implements this interface, the destroy() method is called by the container when the bean is being destroyed (typically during application context shutdown).

    • This is useful for releasing resources like database connections, threads, or file handles.

  2. Custom destroyMethod in @Bean or XML config

    • You can specify a destroy method without implementing an interface:

      @Bean(destroyMethod = "cleanup")
public MyService myService() {
    return new MyService();
}

    • Spring will invoke the cleanup() method when destroying the bean.

  3. @PreDestroy annotation (JSR-250 standard)

    • A method annotated with @PreDestroy is called before the bean is removed from the context.

    • Example:

      @PreDestroy
public void shutdown() {
    System.out.println("Releasing resources...");
}

For comparison with initialization phase:

  • @PostConstruct and afterPropertiesSet() (from InitializingBean) are used for custom initialization logic.

results.question_plain: Which of the following methods is used for customizing the destruction phase of a bean's lifecycle and is part of the DisposableBean interface in Spring?

prompt.question:
Fill in the 3 blanks.

The following describe the components of the MVC design pattern. The ______ provides the representation to the user. It gets the information from the ______. The third component, the ______, can inform the other 2 MVC parts to update their state (data store or representation).
feedbacks: The controller is responsible for handling user input and updating the model and view accordingly. It acts as the intermediary between the model and the view. The model represents the data and business logic of the application, while the view is responsible for presenting the data to the user. Therefore, the correct order is controller, model, view.

In the MVC design pattern, the model represents the data and business logic of the application. The view provides the representation to the user by presenting the data from the model. The controller acts as the intermediary between the model and the view, handling user input and updating the model and view. Therefore, the correct order is model, view, controller.

The view is responsible for providing the representation to the user by presenting the data from the model. The model represents the data and business logic of the application. The controller acts as the intermediary between the model and the view, handling user input and updating the model and view. Therefore, the correct order is view, model, controller.

The model represents the data and business logic of the application. The controller is responsible for handling user input and updating the model and view accordingly. The view provides the representation to the user by presenting the data from the model. Therefore, the correct order is model, controller, view.
prompt.explanation:
The Model-View-Controller (MVC) design pattern is a fundamental architectural pattern used in software development, especially in web frameworks like Spring MVC. It separates concerns into three distinct components, improving maintainability, scalability, and testability:

  1. View

    • Provides the representation to the user.

    • Responsible for displaying data (UI) and sending user actions (input) to the controller.

    • Example in Spring MVC: JSP, Thymeleaf, or HTML pages.

  2. Model

    • Represents the data and business logic of the application.

    • Encapsulates the application’s state and rules for manipulating it.

    • Example in Spring MVC: Java classes like User, Product, or service/data layer beans.

  3. Controller

    • Acts as the intermediary between the View and the Model.

    • Handles user input, processes requests, updates the Model, and selects the View to render.

    • Example in Spring MVC: classes annotated with @Controller that handle HTTP requests.

Correct fill-in order:

  • View → Model → Controller

Meaning:

  • The View provides the representation.

  • It gets its information from the Model.

  • The Controller manages communication between the two, updating either the model’s data or the view’s representation based on user interaction.

prompt.question: Which exception can potentially be thrown when calling the JdbcTemplate.query() method in Spring?
feedbacks: SQLException is a general exception related to database access and manipulation errors. While it is possible for a SQLException to be thrown when using JdbcTemplate.query(), it is not the specific exception that is typically thrown by this method in the Spring framework.

QueryException is not a standard exception in the Spring framework. While it may be used in other contexts or frameworks, it is not the exception that is typically thrown when calling the JdbcTemplate.query() method in Spring.

JdbcException is not a standard exception in the Spring framework. While it may be related to JDBC operations, it is not the exception that is typically thrown by the JdbcTemplate.query() method in Spring.

DataAccessException is the correct exception that can potentially be thrown when calling the JdbcTemplate.query() method in Spring. DataAccessException is a common exception in the Spring framework that is used to handle database access and manipulation errors, making it the most appropriate choice for this scenario.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring, database operations are commonly performed using the JdbcTemplate class, which provides a simplified API for executing SQL queries and updates. Unlike plain JDBC, Spring handles much of the boilerplate code (such as resource management and exception handling) automatically.

One of the key improvements Spring provides over raw JDBC is exception translation:

  • In plain JDBC, methods throw checked exceptions like SQLException.

  • In Spring, instead of exposing SQLException directly, these are caught and translated into runtime exceptions that are part of the DataAccessException hierarchy.

This design offers several benefits:

  1. Consistent API → Developers deal with a unified set of unchecked exceptions, rather than database-specific checked exceptions.

  2. Database independence → Spring translates vendor-specific error codes into well-defined exceptions (e.g., DuplicateKeyException, DataIntegrityViolationException), making code more portable.

  3. Cleaner code → No need to clutter DAO logic with checked exception handling.

Example:

List<Employee> employees = jdbcTemplate.query(
    "SELECT * FROM employees WHERE department_id = ?",
    new Object[]{10},
    (rs, rowNum) -> new Employee(rs.getInt("id"), rs.getString("name"))
);

If something goes wrong (e.g., invalid SQL, constraint violation), Spring may throw:

  • DataAccessException (base class).

  • Or a more specific subclass like BadSqlGrammarException, DataIntegrityViolationException, etc.

results.question_plain: Which exception can potentially be thrown when calling the JdbcTemplate.query() method in Spring?

prompt.question: Fill in the blanks. ________ is an implementation of ________ for a single JPA EntityManagerFactory. It binds a JPA EntityManager from the mentioned factory to the thread.
feedbacks: JpaTemplate is not an implementation of transaction management for a single JPA EntityManagerFactory. It is used for simplifying JPA data access code but does not bind an EntityManager to a thread.

JpaTransactionManager is the correct choice as it is an implementation of transaction management for a single JPA EntityManagerFactory. It binds an EntityManager to the thread using PlatformTransactionManager interface, ensuring proper transaction handling.
JpaDataSource is not related to binding an EntityManager to a thread in JPA. It is typically used for configuring the data source for JPA persistence units.
JpaEntity is not an implementation of transaction management or EntityManager binding in JPA. It represents an entity class in JPA that maps to a database table.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring, transaction management is unified under the PlatformTransactionManager interface, which provides a consistent programming model for different persistence technologies (JDBC, JPA, Hibernate, JTA, etc.). Each technology has its own specialized implementation.

For JPA, the implementation is JpaTransactionManager.

  • JpaTransactionManager:

    • Provides transaction management for a single EntityManagerFactory.

    • Binds a JPA EntityManager to the current thread, making it available for DAOs and repository methods within the same transaction scope.

    • Works seamlessly with @Transactional annotations to demarcate transaction boundaries.

  • How it works:

    1. When a transaction begins, Spring creates or retrieves an EntityManager from the EntityManagerFactory.

    2. This EntityManager is bound to the current thread so that all DAOs/repositories within that thread share the same persistence context.

    3. At transaction commit or rollback, Spring flushes changes and closes the EntityManager.

This mechanism ensures consistent data access across repositories in the same transaction.

results.question_plain: Fill in the blanks. ________ is an implementation of ________ for a single JPA EntityManagerFactory. It binds a JPA EntityManager from the mentioned factory to the thread.

prompt.question: Which of the following are valid usages of the @Profile annotation in Spring?
feedbacks: The @Profile annotation in Spring allows for the conditional loading of beans based on the specified profiles. In this choice, the MyService bean will only be created when the "dev" profile is active, making it a valid usage of the @Profile annotation.

The usage of @Profile with the "exclude" attribute is not valid in Spring. The correct syntax should be @Profile("!production") to exclude the "production" profile, not @Profile({exclude="production"}).

This choice demonstrates a valid usage of the @Profile annotation in Spring. The MyConfig class will be loaded only when the profile is not "production," allowing for conditional bean creation based on the active profile.

This choice showcases another valid usage of the @Profile annotation in Spring. The MyService bean will be created when either the "dev" or "staging" profiles are active, providing flexibility in bean creation based on different profiles.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring, the @Profile annotation is used to conditionally enable or disable beans (or entire configuration classes) based on the currently active environment profiles. Profiles allow applications to adapt their behavior and configuration for different environments, such as development, testing, staging, or production.

Key points about @Profile:

  1. Applied to beans or configuration classes

    • You can put @Profile on individual @Bean methods or on a whole @Configuration class.

    • If placed on a class, all beans defined within that class are conditionally loaded.

  2. Profile activation

    • Profiles are activated by setting the property spring.profiles.active (for example, in application.properties, environment variables, or JVM system properties).

    • Example:

      spring.profiles.active=dev

  3. Syntax for multiple profiles

    • @Profile({"dev", "staging"}) → The bean is active if any of the listed profiles is active.

    • @Profile("!production") → The bean is active if the production profile is not active.

  4. Invalid usage

    • There is no exclude attribute. Excluding a profile must be expressed with the ! syntax.

Examples of valid usage:

  • On a bean:

    @Configuration
public class MyConfig {
    @Bean
    @Profile("dev")
    public MyService myService() {
        return new MyService();
    }
}

  • On a class with negation:

    @Profile("!production")
@Configuration
public class MyConfig {
    // beans here will only load if not in production
}

  • With multiple profiles:

    @Configuration
public class MyConfig {
    @Bean
    @Profile({"dev","staging"})
    public MyService myService() {
        return new MyService();
    }
}
answers:
@Configuration 
public class MyConfig { 

    @Profile({"dev"}) 
    @Bean
    public MyService myService(){
    }
}

@Configuration 
public class MyConfig {
 
    @Profile({"dev","staging"}) 
    @Bean public MyService myService(){}
}

results.question_plain: Which of the following are valid usages of the @Profile annotation in Spring?

prompt.question: Which of the following are the protocols that can be used to access actuator endpoints?
feedbacks: IMAP is a protocol used for email retrieval, not for accessing actuator endpoints in a Spring application. It is not relevant to the management and monitoring of the application's operational aspects.

HTTP is a protocol commonly used to access actuator endpoints in a Spring application. It allows for the retrieval of operational information and management of the application through standardized endpoints.

JMX (Java Management Extensions) is a protocol that can be used to access actuator endpoints in a Spring application. It provides a way to monitor and manage Java applications, including Spring applications, through a set of standardized interfaces.

UDP (User Datagram Protocol) is a connectionless protocol that is not typically used for accessing actuator endpoints in a Spring application. It is more commonly used for communication that does not require reliable delivery or ordering of data.
prompt.explanation:
Spring Boot Actuator provides production-ready features for monitoring and managing applications. These include endpoints that expose runtime information such as health, metrics, environment, and more. To make these endpoints accessible, Spring Boot supports multiple access protocols:

  1. HTTP

    • The most common way to expose actuator endpoints.

    • Endpoints are available under a configurable base path (default: /actuator).

    • Example:

      GET http://localhost:8080/actuator/health
GET http://localhost:8080/actuator/metrics

    • Security can be configured to restrict access to sensitive endpoints.

  2. JMX (Java Management Extensions)

    • Provides access to actuator endpoints as MBeans.

    • Useful for integration with monitoring tools like JConsole or enterprise management systems.

    • JMX endpoints can be enabled/disabled in configuration (management.endpoints.jmx.exposure).

  3. Not supported protocols

    • IMAP: An email protocol, unrelated to monitoring or managing applications.

    • UDP: A lightweight transport protocol, not suitable for structured management and monitoring of actuator endpoints.

results.question_plain: Which of the following are the protocols that can be used to access actuator endpoints?

prompt.question: Does the ApplicationContext interface extend AutoCloseable in Spring?
feedbacks:
This is incorrect. While it is true that the ApplicationContext interface in Spring does not extend AutoCloseable, answering “True” to the question is wrong.

  • ApplicationContext does not inherit the close() method from AutoCloseable.

  • However, its subinterface ConfigurableApplicationContext does extend Closeable (which extends AutoCloseable), making it usable in try-with-resources blocks.

False. The ApplicationContext interface in Spring does not extend the AutoCloseable interface. This means that the ApplicationContext interface does not have the close() method defined by the AutoCloseable interface, and therefore cannot be directly used in a try-with-resources block for automatic resource management.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring, the ApplicationContext is the central interface for accessing the Spring container, providing configuration, bean creation, and dependency injection. However, when it comes to resource management and cleanup, there is an important distinction between ApplicationContext and its subinterfaces.

  1. ApplicationContext interface

    • Does not extend AutoCloseable.

    • It is designed as a read-only container for beans at runtime.

    • Because it doesn’t implement AutoCloseable, it cannot be directly used in a try-with-resources block.

  2. ConfigurableApplicationContext interface

    • A subinterface of ApplicationContext.

    • Does extend both Lifecycle and Closeable (which itself extends AutoCloseable).

    • This means ConfigurableApplicationContext can be explicitly closed using close(), releasing resources such as bean destroy methods or shutdown hooks.

    Example:

    try (ConfigurableApplicationContext context =
        SpringApplication.run(MyApplication.class, args)) {
    // use beans from context
} // context.close() is called automatically here

  3. Practical implication

    • If you only reference ApplicationContext, you cannot close it explicitly.

    • If you need to manage the lifecycle (e.g., in standalone or integration tests), you should use ConfigurableApplicationContext.

prompt.question: Which of the following statements about PlatformTransactionManager in Spring are correct?
feedbacks: A bean of type PlatformTransactionManager can indeed be used to imperatively manage transactions in a Spring application. It provides the necessary methods to begin, commit, or rollback transactions as needed.

It is not mandatory to have only one bean of type PlatformTransactionManager in your application. You can have multiple instances of PlatformTransactionManager beans, each serving a different purpose or managing transactions for different data sources.
PlatformTransactionManager is not a class but an interface in Spring. It defines the methods that a transaction manager must implement to support transaction management in a Spring application.

A bean of type PlatformTransactionManager is indeed used behind the scenes when the @Transactional annotation is used in Spring. The Spring framework automatically detects the PlatformTransactionManager bean and uses it to manage transactions for methods annotated with @Transactional.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring, PlatformTransactionManager is the central interface for transaction management. It abstracts away the underlying transaction implementation, allowing Spring to support multiple persistence technologies (JDBC, JPA, Hibernate, JTA, etc.) with a unified programming model.

Key points:

  1. Interface, not a class

    • PlatformTransactionManager is an interface in Spring (org.springframework.transaction.PlatformTransactionManager).

    • It defines methods like getTransaction(), commit(), and rollback() that concrete transaction managers must implement.

  2. Imperative transaction management

    • Developers can use it directly to programmatically manage transactions. Example:

      @Autowired
private PlatformTransactionManager txManager;

public void doWork() {
    TransactionStatus status = txManager.getTransaction(new DefaultTransactionDefinition());
    try {
        // business logic
        txManager.commit(status);
    } catch (Exception e) {
        txManager.rollback(status);
        throw e;
    }
}

  3. Declarative transaction management

    • In practice, developers rarely call it directly.

    • Instead, they use the @Transactional annotation, and Spring automatically delegates to the configured PlatformTransactionManager behind the scenes.

  4. Multiple beans are allowed

    • You can define more than one PlatformTransactionManager in the same application (for example, if you have multiple data sources).

    • In such cases, you may need to qualify them explicitly using @Qualifier or configure transaction management per data source.

  5. Common implementations

    • DataSourceTransactionManager → for JDBC.

    • JpaTransactionManager → for JPA.

    • HibernateTransactionManager → for Hibernate.

    • JtaTransactionManager → for JTA in distributed transactions.
answers: A bean of type PlatformTransactionManager can be used to imperatively manage transactions
Only one bean of type PlatformTransactionManager must be present in your application
A bean of type PlatformTransactionManager is used behind the curtains from @Transactional

results.question_plain: Which of the following statements about PlatformTransactionManager in Spring are correct?

prompt.question: Which of the following statements about the @Query annotation in Spring Data are true?
feedbacks: Queries defined using @NamedQuery will not take precedence over queries annotated to the query method using @Query. @NamedQuery is used for JPQL queries defined at the entity level, while @Query is used for defining custom queries at the repository method level.
The @Query annotation allows the execution of native queries in Spring Data by setting the ‘nativeQuery’ flag to true. This enables the use of SQL queries directly in the annotation.

The execution of pagination or dynamic sorting for native queries is not supported when using the @Query annotation in Spring Data. This limitation is important to consider when working with native queries that require pagination or sorting functionality.

Named queries declared in orm.xml will not take precedence over queries annotated to the query method using @Query. The @Query annotation provides a way to define custom queries directly at the repository method level, offering more flexibility and control over query execution.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring Data JPA, the @Query annotation is used to define custom queries directly on repository interface methods. By default, Spring can derive queries from method names, but @Query gives developers full control by letting them write the exact JPQL or SQL they want executed.

Key points about @Query:

  1. JPQL by Default

    • Queries written inside @Query are interpreted as JPQL (Java Persistence Query Language).

    • Example:

      @Query("SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.active = true")
List<User> findActiveUsers();

  2. Native Queries

    • By setting nativeQuery = true, developers can execute raw SQL queries directly.

    • Example:

      @Query(value = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE active = true", nativeQuery = true)
List<User> findActiveUsersNative();

    • This is useful when JPQL isn’t enough or when you need database-specific features.

  3. Pagination and Sorting Limitations

    • Spring Data JPA does not support dynamic sorting or automatic pagination for native queries out of the box.

    • While JPQL queries and derived queries can handle Pageable and Sort parameters automatically, native queries require extra effort (e.g., providing a countQuery explicitly for pagination).

    • Dynamic sorting (Sort sort) simply isn’t supported with native queries.

  4. Precedence Rules

    • If both a @NamedQuery (defined on an entity or in orm.xml) and a @Query annotation exist for the same repository method, the @Query definition takes precedence.

    • This means repository-level queries are more specific and override globally defined ones, giving developers more flexibility.
answers: Queries defined using @NamedQuery will take precedence over queries annotated to the query method using @Query.
The @Query annotation allows to execute native queries by setting the ‘nativeQuery’ flag to true.
The execution of pagination or dynamic sorting for native queries is not supported.
Named queries declared in orm.xml will take precedence over queries annotated to the query method using @Query.

results.question_plain: Which of the following statements about the @Query annotation in Spring Data are true?

prompt.question:
Given the following Spring configuration file, which of the following statements about bean naming and referencing are correct?


@Configuration
public class AppConfig {

    @Bean
    public MyServiceImpl myServiceImpl() {
        return new MyServiceImpl();
    }

    @Bean
    public JpaDao jpaDaoBean() {
        return new JpaDao();
    }
}
feedbacks: In Spring, when a bean is declared using the @Bean annotation, the method name itself serves as the default bean name unless explicitly specified using the 'name' attribute. Therefore, the bean named 'MyServiceImpl' can be referenced by its method name 'myServiceImpl' without the need for an additional id.

The bean named 'jpaDaoBean' will have the default bean name equal to the method name 'jpaDaoBean'. In this case, the bean will be referenced by the name 'jpaDaoBean' unless a different name is explicitly provided.

In Spring, when beans are declared using the @Bean annotation, the method names themselves serve as the default bean names. Therefore, both 'MyServiceImpl' and 'JpaDao' beans can be referenced by their respective method names 'myServiceImpl' and 'jpaDaoBean' without the need for additional ids.

While it is common practice to follow naming conventions for beans in Spring, the absence of explicit naming conventions does not impact the functionality of the beans. The beans 'MyServiceImpl' and 'JpaDao' can still be referenced by their method names without any issues.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring, when defining beans using @Bean methods inside a @Configuration class, the default bean name is derived from the method name. This means that unless explicitly overridden, the name of the bean in the application context will be the same as the method that creates it.

1. Default Bean Names

  • For the method:

    @Bean
public MyServiceImpl myServiceImpl() {
    return new MyServiceImpl();
}

    The bean name is "myServiceImpl" (the method name).

  • For the method:

    @Bean
public JpaDao jpaDaoBean() {
    return new JpaDao();
}

    The bean name is "jpaDaoBean".

This is true regardless of the class name of the bean being created. What matters is the method name, not the return type’s class name.

2. Overriding Bean Names

You can override the default bean name by providing a custom name in the annotation:

@Bean(name = "customService")
public MyServiceImpl myServiceImpl() {
    return new MyServiceImpl();
}

In this case, the bean would be referenced by "customService".

3. Misconceptions

  • It is not required to specify an id or name for beans declared with @Bean. The method name works perfectly as the identifier.

  • The bean name is not derived from the class name (MyServiceImpl or JpaDao).
answers: The first declared bean MyServiceImpl is missing an id must be named myService
The JpaDao type bean will have name equal to "jpaDao"
Both beans can be referenced by their method names without specifying ids.
Both beans are missing proper naming conventions and should be renamed.

prompt.question: Spring Boot can auto-configure embedded H2, HSQL, and Derby databases. What do you need to provide to make this happen?
feedbacks: By including a build dependency to the embedded database that you want to use in your Spring Boot project, the auto-configuration mechanism of Spring Boot will automatically detect the presence of the embedded database and configure it for use without any additional manual configuration.

Setting spring.data.embeded.autoconfigure to true is not a valid configuration option in Spring Boot for auto-configuring embedded databases. The correct way to enable auto-configuration for embedded databases is by including the necessary build dependency in your project.

Providing a connection URL is not required to enable auto-configuration of embedded databases in Spring Boot. The auto-configuration mechanism handles the setup of the embedded database without the need for manual connection URL configuration.

Explicitly configuring a DataSource bean in your application context is not necessary when using Spring Boot's auto-configuration for embedded databases. The auto-configuration mechanism takes care of setting up the DataSource for the embedded database based on the dependencies included in the project.
prompt.explanation:
Spring Boot provides strong support for embedded databases like H2, HSQLDB, and Derby, making it very easy to get started without needing to manually configure a DataSource.

1. How Auto-Configuration Works

  • Spring Boot checks the classpath for certain libraries.

  • If it finds an embedded database dependency (like H2), and no custom DataSource bean has been defined, it will:

    • Create an in-memory database instance automatically.

    • Configure a DataSource bean pointing to it.

    • Even initialize schema/data from schema.sql and data.sql if those files exist on the classpath.

2. What You Need to Provide

  • Only the dependency in your build file. Nothing else.

  • Example (Maven with H2):

    <dependency>
    <groupId>com.h2database</groupId>
    <artifactId>h2</artifactId>
    <scope>runtime</scope>
</dependency>

  • Example (Gradle with H2):

    runtimeOnly 'com.h2database:h2'

Once the dependency is present, Spring Boot’s auto-configuration sets up the database for you.

3. What You Do NOT Need

  • ❌ No manual DataSource bean declaration.

  • ❌ No special property like spring.data.embedded.autoconfigure=true (this does not exist).

  • ❌ No need to provide a connection URL (Spring Boot generates one by default, e.g., jdbc:h2:mem:testdb).

4. When Auto-Configuration Is Disabled

  • If you define your own DataSource bean, Spring Boot backs off and does not create an embedded database automatically.

  • If multiple embedded databases are on the classpath, Boot fails with an error (ambiguity) unless you specify one explicitly.

5. Practical Use

Embedded databases are typically used for:

  • Local development.

  • Unit/integration testing.

  • Prototyping apps quickly.

For production, you would usually configure a real database (MySQL, PostgreSQL, etc.).
answers: You need only to include a build dependency to the embedded database that you want to use
You need to provide a connection URL
You need to explicitly configure a DataSource bean in your application context.

results.question_plain: Spring Boot can auto-configure embedded H2, HSQL, and Derby databases. What do you need to provide to make this happen?

prompt.question: Which are valid ways to inject values using @Value annotation? There is a valid environment property my.special.property already registered
links: [Using Environment Variables in Spring Boot’s Properties Files](https://www.baeldung.com/spring-boot-properties-env-variables)
feedbacks: The correct syntax to inject the value of the environment property "my.special.property" is by using '#' symbol.
The syntax "$( ... )" is not valid for injecting values using the @Value annotation. The correct syntax to reference environment properties is "${...}".
This choice is correct as it uses the correct syntax "${...}" to inject the value of the environment property "my.special.property" using the @Value annotation.
The syntax "#(...)" is not valid for injecting values using the @Value annotation. The correct syntax to reference environment properties is "${...}".
prompt.explanation:
In Spring, the @Value annotation is used to inject values into fields, method parameters, or constructor arguments. These values can come from different sources, such as:

  • Environment properties (application.properties, application.yml, system properties, environment variables).

  • Spring Expression Language (SpEL) expressions.

  • Static values (like strings, numbers).

1. Property Placeholder Syntax

The most common way to inject configuration values is with the ${...} placeholder syntax:

@Value("${my.special.property}")
private String property;

Here, my.special.property will be resolved from the Spring Environment (e.g., application.properties).

2. Using SpEL (Spring Expression Language)

You can also use SpEL with the #{...} syntax. For example:

@Value("#{environment['my.special.property']}")
private String property;

This is more flexible than the placeholder syntax because it allows access to the Spring Environment object and supports more complex expressions.

3. When to Use Which

  • Use ${...} when you simply need to inject a property value (most common).

  • Use #{...} when you need access to the environment or want to combine values dynamically with SpEL.

prompt.question: Which of the following are core steps in the Spring bean lifecycle according to the official documentation?
feedbacks: According to the official documentation, the first core step in the Spring bean lifecycle is the instantiation of the bean by the Spring container. This step involves creating an instance of the bean based on the bean definition provided in the application context.

Another core step in the Spring bean lifecycle, as outlined in the official documentation, is the injection of dependencies into the bean. This step, known as property population, involves setting the values of the bean's properties based on the dependencies defined in the bean configuration.

The official documentation states that the next core step in the Spring bean lifecycle is the invocation of the initialization callback method. This method, such as afterPropertiesSet() or a custom init method defined by the developer, is called after the bean has been instantiated and its dependencies have been injected.

The manual registration of a bean by the developer in the application context is not listed as one of the core steps in the Spring bean lifecycle according to the official documentation. The Spring container is responsible for managing the lifecycle of beans, including their instantiation, dependency injection, and initialization.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring, every bean managed by the IoC (Inversion of Control) container follows a well-defined lifecycle. According to the official documentation, the process begins when the container instantiates the bean based on its configuration (via XML, Java config, or annotations). After instantiation, Spring performs dependency injection (property population), ensuring that all required collaborators and configuration values are properly set.

Once dependencies are injected, Spring calls any initialization callbacks defined by the bean. This could be the afterPropertiesSet() method from the InitializingBean interface, a method annotated with @PostConstruct, or a custom init method declared in the configuration. These callbacks allow developers to run custom logic once the bean is fully prepared but before it is used in the application.

While beans can be registered manually, this is not considered a core step in the lifecycle. Instead, the container automatically manages beans and their lifecycle phases. The official lifecycle also includes a destruction phase (not asked in this question), where methods such as destroy() or those annotated with @PreDestroy can be executed when the application context is closed.

This lifecycle ensures that beans are consistently created, wired, and prepared for use, while also giving developers hooks for initialization and cleanup.
answers: The bean is instantiated by the Spring container.
Dependencies are injected into the bean (property population).
The initialization callback method (e.g., afterPropertiesSet() or a custom init method) is invoked.
The bean is manually registered by the developer in the application context.

results.question_plain: Which of the following are core steps in the Spring bean lifecycle according to the official documentation?

prompt.question: Which of the following annotations will be enabled by the usage of @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity(jsr250Enabled = true) on top of your @Configuration annotated class?
feedbacks: The @Secured annotation is not enabled by using @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity(jsr250Enabled = true). This annotation is typically enabled by using @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity(securedEnabled = true) instead.
The @RolesAllowed annotation is enabled by using @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity(jsr250Enabled = true). This annotation allows you to restrict access to methods based on the roles allowed to access them.

The @PreAuthorize annotation is not enabled by using @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity(jsr250Enabled = true). This annotation is typically enabled by using @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity(prePostEnabled = true) instead.

Only the @RolesAllowed annotation is enabled by using @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity(jsr250Enabled = true), not all of the above annotations. To enable all three annotations (@Secured, @RolesAllowed, @PreAuthorize), you would need to use @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity(prePostEnabled = true, securedEnabled = true, jsr250Enabled = true).
prompt.explanation:
In Spring Security, method-level security can be enabled using the @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity annotation placed on a @Configuration class. This annotation activates different types of method security depending on the attributes you specify.

When you set jsr250Enabled = true, Spring enables the JSR-250 annotations, specifically @RolesAllowed. This annotation allows you to restrict method access based on user roles, using role names as defined in your security configuration. For example, applying @RolesAllowed("ADMIN") to a method ensures that only users with the ADMIN role can invoke it.

It is important to note that other method security annotations require different flags:

  • @Secured is enabled via @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity(securedEnabled = true)

  • @PreAuthorize and @PostAuthorize are enabled via @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity(prePostEnabled = true)

By combining these attributes, you can enable multiple annotation styles at once. For instance:

@EnableGlobalMethodSecurity(
    prePostEnabled = true,
    securedEnabled = true,
    jsr250Enabled = true
)

This would allow you to use @PreAuthorize, @Secured, and @RolesAllowed in the same application.

In summary, jsr250Enabled = true specifically unlocks @RolesAllowed, aligning Spring Security with the JSR-250 standard for role-based access control.

results.question_plain: Which of the following annotations will be enabled by the usage of @EnableGlobalMethodSecurity(jsr250Enabled = true) on top of your @Configuration annotated class?

prompt.question: Which of the following AOP advice types can choose to prevent execution of the advised method, and instead return another value?
feedbacks: The @Around advice type in AOP allows the developer to intercept the method execution and control whether the advised method should be executed or not. It can also modify the return value of the advised method, making it suitable for preventing the execution of the advised method and returning another value instead.

The @Before advice type in AOP is used to perform actions before the advised method is executed. It does not have the capability to prevent the execution of the advised method or return another value in its place.

The @AfterThrowing advice type in AOP is triggered after the advised method throws an exception. It is used for handling exceptions and does not have the functionality to prevent the execution of the advised method or return a different value.

The @AfterReturning advice type in AOP is executed after the advised method successfully returns a value. It is used for performing actions after the method execution and does not have the ability to prevent the execution of the advised method or return a different value.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring AOP, different advice types allow you to insert behavior at various points in a method’s execution. Most advice types — such as @Before, @AfterReturning, or @AfterThrowing — simply allow you to run additional logic around the execution of the advised method but do not control whether that method is executed.

The exception is @Around advice, which is the most powerful form of advice. With @Around, you receive a ProceedingJoinPoint object, which gives you explicit control over whether the target method should be executed. By calling proceed(), you allow the method to run, and by skipping it, you can completely bypass the method execution. This makes @Around advice unique because it can:

  • Prevent execution of the advised method.

  • Modify the input arguments before passing them to the target method.

  • Replace the return value with a different one.

  • Wrap method execution with custom behavior such as logging, performance monitoring, or security checks.

For example, you could implement caching logic with @Around advice: if a cached result exists, you skip proceed() and return the cached value instead.

This fine-grained control is why @Around is often used for cross-cutting concerns that require conditional method execution, whereas other advice types are better suited for simple, declarative enhancements.

results.question_plain: Which of the following AOP advice types can choose to prevent execution of the advised method, and instead return another value?

prompt.question: Which of these advices, when used, will have a chance to execute some code before the join point is executed?
feedbacks: @AfterReturning advice is executed after the join point has successfully completed and returned a value. It is not used to execute code before the join point is executed, so it is not the correct choice for this scenario.

@Before advice is specifically designed to execute code before the join point is executed. It allows you to perform actions or set up conditions before the actual method execution takes place, making it a suitable choice for executing code before the join point.

@Around advice allows you to execute code both before and after the join point is executed. It provides the most flexibility compared to other advice types, as it allows you to control the execution flow around the join point. This makes it a valid choice for executing code before the join point.

@After advice is executed after the join point has completed its execution, regardless of the outcome. It is not used to execute code before the join point is executed, so it is not the correct choice for this scenario.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring AOP, different advice types determine when additional logic is executed in relation to a join point (typically a method execution). If you want code to run before the target method is invoked, two types of advice can achieve this:

  • @Before advice — This is the simplest way to insert logic before the join point. The method annotated with @Before executes just before the target method runs. It cannot prevent execution of the method or modify its return value; it is purely for pre-processing, logging, validation, or setting up required context.

  • @Around advice — This is the most flexible advice type. It wraps the join point and allows you to execute logic both before and after the method execution. With @Around, you can even decide not to execute the target method by skipping the proceed() call. Because it runs before the join point, it is also valid when considering pre-execution logic.

Other advice types, such as @After, @AfterReturning, and @AfterThrowing, only execute after the join point has completed and therefore cannot run code before method execution.

In practice:

  • Use @Before for simple, always-run setup code.

  • Use @Around when you need more control (e.g., performance monitoring, caching, or conditional execution).

results.question_plain: Which of these advices, when used, will have a chance to execute some code before the join point is executed?

prompt.question: During which phase can BeanPostProcessor modify the bean instances?
feedbacks: BeanPostProcessor cannot modify bean instances before they are instantiated by the Spring IoC container. This phase is too early in the bean lifecycle for BeanPostProcessor to interact with the beans.

BeanPostProcessor can modify bean instances before the initialization phase of the bean. This is the correct phase for BeanPostProcessor to intercept and modify the bean before it is fully initialized.

BeanPostProcessor does not have the ability to modify bean instances during the bean's dependency injection phase. This phase is focused on injecting dependencies into the bean, not modifying the bean itself.

BeanPostProcessor is not involved in modifying bean instances only during the destruction phase of the bean. This phase occurs after the bean has been fully initialized and used, so BeanPostProcessor does not have the opportunity to modify the bean at this stage.
prompt.explanation:
In the Spring bean lifecycle, a BeanPostProcessor provides a powerful mechanism to intercept and modify beans after they have been instantiated and their dependencies injected, but before and after their initialization phase. Specifically:

  1. After instantiation and dependency injection, but before initialization

    • The postProcessBeforeInitialization() method is called.

    • At this stage, you can wrap, modify, or prepare the bean instance right before any initialization callbacks (such as @PostConstruct, afterPropertiesSet(), or custom init methods) are executed.

  2. After initialization

    • The postProcessAfterInitialization() method is called.

    • Here you can further modify the bean instance, such as wrapping it in a proxy (often done in AOP scenarios) or adding additional behavior after the bean is fully initialized.

Importantly, BeanPostProcessor does not run before instantiation, during dependency injection itself, or during destruction. Instead, it is specifically tied to the initialization phase of the bean lifecycle, giving developers hooks to inject cross-cutting behavior, proxies, or enhancements into beans right as they become ready for use in the application context.

This makes BeanPostProcessor essential for advanced Spring features like AOP proxies, annotation processing, and framework extensions.
answers: Before the bean is instantiated by the Spring IoC container.
Before the initialization phase of the bean.
Only during the destruction phase of the bean.

results.question_plain: During which phase can BeanPostProcessor modify the bean instances?

prompt.question: Which of the following is not amongst the Health Indicator statuses provided out of the box?

The "UP" health indicator status is provided out of the box to indicate that the component is functioning correctly and is in a healthy state. This status is commonly used to show that a component is operational.

The "MAINTENANCE" health indicator status is not provided out of the box as a standard health indicator status. This status is typically used to indicate that a component is undergoing maintenance or is temporarily unavailable for use.

The "OUT_OF_SERVICE" health indicator status is provided out of the box to indicate that a component is not in service and is unavailable. This status is commonly used to show that a component is intentionally taken out of service for maintenance or other reasons.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring Boot Actuator, health indicators provide the status of different parts of the application (like databases, message brokers, or custom subsystems). These statuses are standardized so that monitoring systems can easily interpret them.

Out of the box, Spring Boot defines the following core statuses:

  • UP – The component or the whole application is running and available.

  • DOWN – The component or the whole application is not available or failing.

  • OUT_OF_SERVICE – The component is intentionally taken out of service (for example, during maintenance or scaling down).

  • UNKNOWN – The health status cannot be determined.

The option MAINTENANCE is not one of the predefined statuses. While developers can define custom statuses (including MAINTENANCE if desired), it is not provided by Spring Boot Actuator by default.
