prompt.question: Which of the following best describes the behavior of the HTTP PUT method?
feedbacks: Correct, PUT replaces the resource or creates it if absent and is idempotent.
Incorrect, partial updates are handled by PATCH.
Incorrect, DELETE removes a resource.
Incorrect, this is a vague POST-like description that lacks the idempotent semantics of PUT.
prompt.explanation:
The HTTP PUT method is one of the core RESTful verbs and has very specific semantics that distinguish it from POST and PATCH. Understanding its behavior and idempotency is key for REST API design and for the Spring exam.

  1. Behavior of PUT (Correct)

    • Replaces the target resource with the payload provided.

    • If the resource does not exist, it can be created at the given URI.

    • Example:

      PUT /users/123
{ "id": 123, "name": "mk", "role": "ADMIN" }

      • If /users/123 exists → it is completely replaced.

      • If /users/123 does not exist → it is created.

  2. Idempotency

    • PUT is idempotent: multiple identical PUT requests have the same effect as one.

    • Example: calling PUT /users/123 with the same payload three times produces the same final state.

  3. What PUT is NOT

    • Not for partial updates → use PATCH instead.

    • Not for creating new resources without specifying the URI → that’s POST.

    • Not for deletion → that’s DELETE.

  4. Best Practices

    • Use PUT when the client knows the full URI of the resource and wants to fully replace it.

    • Use POST for creating new resources where the server decides the identifier.

    • Use PATCH for partial updates (e.g., updating just one field of an object).

    • Ensure PUT handlers in Spring controllers are idempotent and replace the entire resource:

      @PutMapping("/users/{id}")
public ResponseEntity<UserDto> updateUser(@PathVariable Long id, @RequestBody UserDto dto) {
    UserDto updated = userService.replaceUser(id, dto);
    return ResponseEntity.ok(updated);
}

  5. Exam Tip

    • PUT = Replace or Create + Idempotent.

    • POST = Create + Non-idempotent.

    • PATCH = Partial update.

    • DELETE = Remove.

Suggestion: In real REST APIs, reserve PUT for full resource replacement and ensure idempotency. For updates where only a subset of fields change, always use PATCH to avoid confusion and wasted payload transfers.
answers: Replaces an existing resource or creates a new resource if it does not exist
Sends data to the server to partially update a resource
Deletes a specified resource on the server
Sends data to the server, usually leading to changes or resource creation

results.question_plain: Which of the following best describes the behavior of the HTTP PUT method?

prompt.question:
public interface IAnimal {}

@Component
public class Dog implements IAnimal {}

@Component
public class Cat implements IAnimal {}

@Component
public class Person {
    @Autowired
    private IAnimal animal;
}


Currently, there's an ambiguity for autowiring the animal field in the Person class. Which of the following options can be applied separately to resolve the ambiguity?
feedbacks: Incorrect, changing the field name does not influence Spring’s type-based autowiring.
Correct, marking one bean as `@Primary` resolves ambiguity for type-based injection.
Incorrect, `@Qualifier("animal")` would reference a bean named "animal", which does not exist.Incorrect, `@Qualifier("animal")` would reference a bean named "animal", which does not exist.
Correct, `@Qualifier("cat")` on the field picks the desired implementation explicitly.
prompt.explanation:
When multiple beans match a single injection point by type, Spring can’t choose automatically. Here, IAnimal has two candidates: Dog and Cat. You resolve the ambiguity by either declaring a primary bean or qualifying the target bean.

  1. @Primary (type preference)

    • Mark exactly one candidate as the default for that type:

      @Component
@Primary
class Cat implements IAnimal {}

    • Any @Autowired IAnimal without qualifiers now injects Cat.

    • Works only if there’s exactly one @Primary per type (otherwise another conflict).

  2. @Qualifier (name preference)

    • Specify the exact bean to inject:

      @Component
class Cat implements IAnimal {}

@Component
class Person {
  @Autowired
  @Qualifier("cat") // bean name is class name decapitalized by default
  private IAnimal animal;
}

    • @Qualifier("cat") matches the bean id cat. Using @Qualifier("animal") fails—there’s no bean named animal.

  3. Why renaming the field doesn’t help

    • Spring’s @Autowired resolves by type first. Field name is ignored (unless you use @Resource, which injects by name).

  4. Other valid patterns

    • Constructor injection with @Qualifier:

      @Component
class Person {
  Person(@Qualifier("cat") IAnimal animal) { ... }
}

    • Java config with @Bean names works similarly with qualifiers.

Exam takeaway: Use @Primary to set a default candidate; use @Qualifier on the injection point to pick a specific bean. Changing the field name doesn’t influence @Autowired.

Suggestion: Prefer constructor injection and use @Qualifier at the constructor parameter for clarity and testability; reserve @Primary for a sensible default across the app.
answers: Change the @Autowired variable from animal to cat or dog
Use the @Primary annotation on one of the classes
Use @Qualifier("animal") on top of Dog or Cat

prompt.question:
Which of the following annotations is best described by the text below?

It creates a Mockito Mock, without injecting it.
links: [Mastering Spring Boot Testing with JUnit and Mockito — Quick Start Guide](https://medium.com/@AlexanderObregon/mastering-spring-boot-testing-with-junit-and-mockito-8bec9b4911fc)
feedbacks: Incorrect, `@MockBean` registers a mock in the Spring context.
Correct, `@Mock` simply creates a Mockito mock without injecting it.
Incorrect, `@InjectMocks` builds a concrete class and populates its fields with mocks.
Incorrect, `@BeanMock` is not a Mockito/Spring annotation.
prompt.explanation:
Mockito provides several annotations for creating and wiring mocks in unit tests. Understanding the difference between them is crucial both for the exam and for clean testing practices.

  1. @Mock (Correct Answer)

    • Comes from Mockito.

    • Creates a plain Mockito mock instance of a class or interface.

    • Does not inject it into the Spring context.

    • Example:

      @Mock
private UserRepository userRepository;

    • Used in pure unit tests where you don’t need the Spring container.

  2. @MockBean (Spring Boot specific)

    • Comes from Spring Boot test support.

    • Replaces an existing bean in the Spring application context with a Mockito mock.

    • Useful for integration-style tests (@SpringBootTest, @WebMvcTest) where you want to keep Spring running but replace some collaborators with mocks.

    • Example:

      @MockBean
private UserRepository userRepository;

  3. @InjectMocks

    • Also from Mockito.

    • Creates a real instance of a class and automatically injects mocks into its fields or constructor.

    • Example:

      @InjectMocks
private UserService userService;

  4. @BeanMock

    • ❌ Not a valid Mockito or Spring annotation (trap option).

  5. Best Practices

    • Use @Mock + @InjectMocks for fast unit tests without Spring.

    • Use @MockBean for Spring Boot tests where you want to keep part of the Spring context but mock out certain collaborators.

  6. Exam Tip

    • If the question says “creates a Mockito mock, without injecting it”, the answer is always @Mock.

    • If it says “creates and replaces a Spring bean with a mock”, the answer is @MockBean.

Suggestion: For fast and isolated unit tests, always use @Mock + @InjectMocks outside of Spring. Use @MockBean sparingly, only in Spring Boot slice or integration tests where you need the container.

prompt.question: What is an after returning advice in the context of Aspect-Oriented Programming (AOP) in Spring?
feedbacks: Incorrect, that description matches `@After` (finally) advice.
Incorrect, surrounding behaviour corresponds to `@Around` advice.
Incorrect, that is the definition of `@Before` advice.
Correct, after-returning advice runs only when the join point completes successfully.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring AOP, advice defines when and how cross-cutting logic is applied relative to a join point (usually a method execution). Different types of advice exist, and each has precise semantics:

  1. @Before

    • Runs before the target method execution.

    • Cannot prevent execution, but can prepare context (e.g., logging, security checks).

  2. @AfterReturning (Correct Answer)

    • Runs after the target method executes successfully (i.e., no exception is thrown).

    • Can access the method’s return value and perform logic based on it (e.g., auditing results, post-processing).

    • Example:

      @AfterReturning(
    pointcut = "execution(* com.example.service.*.*(..))",
    returning = "result")
public void logAfterReturning(Object result) {
    System.out.println("Method returned: " + result);
}

  3. @AfterThrowing

    • Runs only if the method throws an exception.

    • Used for exception logging, rollback handling, or monitoring failures.

  4. @After (Finally Advice)

    • Runs after method completion regardless of outcome (success or exception).

    • Equivalent to a finally block in Java.

  5. @Around

    • Wraps the entire method execution.

    • Can control execution (decide whether to proceed, modify arguments, wrap return values).

    • Most powerful advice type.

  6. Best Practices

    • Use @AfterReturning for post-processing only when you need to inspect/transform return values.

    • Keep logic light—avoid heavy operations in advice to prevent slowing down all advised methods.

    • For full control, prefer @Around.

  7. Exam Tip

    • Key phrase: “executes after a method completes without exception” → @AfterReturning.

    • If exceptions are involved → @AfterThrowing.

    • If it always runs → @After.

Suggestion: In real projects, use @AfterReturning for logging or auditing results, but reserve business logic changes for service layers. Keep aspects focused on cross-cutting concerns (logging, security, metrics), not core business behavior.
answers: Advice to be executed regardless of the means by which a join point exits
Advice that surrounds a method invocation and can perform custom behavior before and after the method invocation
Advice to be executed before method invocation
Advice to be executed after a join point completes without throwing an exception

feedbacks:
Correct, this method matches the pointcut because:

  • It is in the com.test.service package.

  • The .. in the pointcut allows matching sub-packages.

  • The method name (transfert) matches the *.* pattern.

  • The method has exactly one parameter, which matches the (*) pattern.
Incorrect, this method does not match the pointcut because it has two parameters, while the pointcut specifies exactly one parameter ((*)).

Correct, this method matches the pointcut because:

  • It is in a sub-package of com.test.service (com.test.service.account).

  • The .. in the pointcut allows matching sub-packages.

  • The method name (transfert) matches the *.* pattern.

  • The method has exactly one parameter, which matches the (*) pattern.
Incorrect, because two of the given method signatures match the pointcut.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring AOP, the execution pointcut designator is used to match method executions based on their signature. The syntax for execution is as follows:

execution(modifiers-pattern? return-type-pattern declaring-type-pattern? method-name-pattern(param-pattern) throws-pattern?)

Pointcut Breakdown: execution(* com.test.service..*.*(*)):

  1. * (return-type-pattern): Matches any return type.

  2. com.test.service..* (declaring-type-pattern): Matches any class in the com.test.service package or its sub-packages.

  3. *.* (method-name-pattern): Matches any method in the class.

  4. (*) (param-pattern): Matches methods with exactly one parameter of any type.

prompt.question: Which design pattern ensures that a set of changes is treated as a single unit so that either all updates succeed or none are applied?
feedbacks: Incorrect, a query merely reads data and has no transactional semantics.
Correct, the Unit of Work pattern batches changes so they can be committed or rolled back together.
Incorrect, a generic request does not imply atomic change tracking.
Incorrect in this design-pattern context—transactions implement all-or-nothing at the database level, but the question is about the application pattern.
prompt.explanation:
The design pattern that ensures multiple changes are treated as a single atomic unit in application code is the Unit of Work pattern. It’s widely used in ORMs like Hibernate and Entity Framework, and it underpins transactional integrity in Spring applications.

  1. Unit of Work (Correct)

    • Tracks all changes to objects during a business transaction.

    • Ensures either all updates succeed (commit) or none apply (rollback).

    • Coordinates inserts, updates, and deletes into a single batch operation.

    • Example: Hibernate’s Session or JPA’s EntityManager acts as a Unit of Work, tracking dirty entities and flushing them together.

  2. Transaction vs Unit of Work

    • Transaction = a database-level concept ensuring ACID properties.

    • Unit of Work = an application-level pattern that groups operations and typically commits them inside a single transaction.

    • Together: Unit of Work coordinates state changes, then wraps them in a transaction to ensure atomicity.

  3. Other Options (Incorrect)

    • Query → read-only operation, no transactional semantics.

    • Request → a generic interaction, doesn’t imply atomicity.

  4. Spring Context

    • In Spring, @Transactional relies on the Unit of Work pattern behind the scenes (via persistence providers like Hibernate).

    • Example:

      @Transactional
public void registerUser(User user) {
    userRepository.save(user);
    profileRepository.createProfile(user);
    // Both changes succeed or both rollback
}

  5. Best Practices

    • Treat business use cases (e.g., “place order”, “register user”) as the boundary for your Unit of Work.

    • Don’t span Units of Work across multiple service calls or remote calls—keep them local and consistent.

    • Use ORM-provided UoW (Hibernate, JPA) instead of rolling your own.

  6. Exam Tip

    • If the question is about design pattern → answer is Unit of Work.

    • If it’s about database behavior → answer is Transaction.

Suggestion: Always align your Unit of Work boundaries with business transactions. In Spring, achieve this with @Transactional at the service layer, ensuring all repository calls inside a method succeed or rollback together.

results.question_plain: Which design pattern ensures that a set of changes is treated as a single unit so that either all updates succeed or none are applied?

prompt.question: In the context of security systems, which process comes first: authentication or authorization?
feedbacks: Correct, systems must authenticate before they can authorise.
Incorrect, authorisation decisions rely on the identity established during authentication.
prompt.explanation:
In security systems, authentication always comes before authorization. The order is fundamental because you can’t decide what a user is allowed to do until you know who the user is.

  1. Authentication (First Step)

    • Confirms the user’s identity (username/password, token, certificate, biometric, etc.).

    • Produces an authenticated principal (in Spring, usually a UserDetails object wrapped inside an Authentication).

    • Example in Spring Security:

      Authentication auth = authenticationManager.authenticate(token);
SecurityContextHolder.getContext().setAuthentication(auth);

  2. Authorization (Second Step)

    • Decides what the authenticated user can access.

    • Based on roles, authorities, or fine-grained permissions.

    • In Spring Security, this is evaluated against access rules (e.g., hasRole("ADMIN")).

  3. Why the Order Matters

    • Without authentication, the system cannot reliably assign permissions.

    • Authorization is meaningless if the identity is unknown or unverified.

  4. Spring Security Context

    • Authentication happens when a request first enters the security filter chain.

    • Authorization happens after, typically in filters or method-level checks (@PreAuthorize, @Secured).

  5. Exam Tip

    • If the question asks “which comes first”, the answer is always Authentication.

    • Authentication → Who are you?

    • Authorization → What are you allowed to do?

Suggestion: In real applications, always separate authentication providers (verifying identity) from authorization logic (checking roles/permissions). This makes your security model cleaner and easier to audit.

results.question_plain: In the context of security systems, which process comes first: authentication or authorization?

prompt.question:
Is the following statement true or false?
When @RequestParam is used without specifying a value, it defaults to using the method parameter name as the request parameter name for binding
feedbacks: Correct, without an explicit value, `@RequestParam` uses the method-parameter name.
Incorrect, the default binding name is the parameter name when unspecified.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring MVC, @RequestParam is used to bind an HTTP request parameter to a method parameter in a controller.

  1. Default Behavior

    • If you provide a value in @RequestParam, that value is used as the request parameter name:

      @GetMapping("/greet")
public String greet(@RequestParam("name") String name) { ... }

      → Binds ?name=mk.

    • If you do not provide a value, Spring uses the method parameter name as the binding name:

      @GetMapping("/greet")
public String greet(@RequestParam String name) { ... }

      → Also binds ?name=mk.

  2. Requirements

    • This works only if the Java compiler stores parameter names in the class file (-parameters flag).

    • If parameter names are not available, Spring cannot infer them, and binding may fail unless you specify the name explicitly.

  3. Optional Parameters

    • You can make a parameter optional:

      @RequestParam(required = false) String name

      → If missing, name is null.

    • Or provide a default value:

      @RequestParam(defaultValue = "Guest") String name

  4. Best Practices

    • Always specify the parameter name in public APIs for clarity, even though it’s optional.

    • Relying on method parameter names requires consistent compiler settings, which might vary between environments.

  5. Exam Tip

    • Statement is True: if no value is given, @RequestParam binds using the method parameter name.

    • Trick: watch for mention of compiler flags (-parameters), since in older projects you may still need explicit values.

prompt.question: Your application contains join points, pointcuts, and advice. What Spring module or feature are you using?
feedbacks: Correct, join points, pointcuts, and advice are AOP constructs.
Incorrect, Spring Data JPA focuses on repositories and persistence.
Incorrect, Spring MVC handles web requests, not AOP.
Incorrect, Spring Security may use AOP internally but the module in question is Spring AOP.
prompt.explanation:
Aspect-Oriented Programming (AOP) in Spring is a core mechanism for handling cross-cutting concerns — functionality that cuts across multiple layers (like logging, transactions, or security). Instead of duplicating code, you modularize these behaviors into aspects. The dedicated module for this in Spring is Spring AOP.

  1. Core AOP Concepts

    • Join Point → A specific point in the execution of the program (typically a method execution in Spring AOP).

    • Pointcut → An expression that matches one or more join points (e.g., "all methods annotated with @Transactional").

    • Advice → The action taken at a matched join point (before, after, around).

    • Aspect → A module that groups advices and pointcuts.

    • Weaving → The process of applying aspects to target objects. In Spring AOP, this happens at runtime using proxies (JDK dynamic proxies or CGLIB).

  2. Spring AOP vs Other Spring Modules

    • Spring AOP (Correct) → Implements AOP concepts directly (join points, pointcuts, advice). This is the exam-relevant module.

    • Spring Data JPA → Provides repository abstraction and query execution, no AOP fundamentals.

    • Spring MVC → Web framework for handling HTTP requests/responses, unrelated to AOP.

    • Spring Security → Uses AOP under the hood (e.g., method security with @PreAuthorize), but AOP itself is not its purpose — it consumes AOP, doesn’t define it.

  3. Best Practices in Real Systems

    • Use AOP for cross-cutting concerns only — logging, transaction management, performance monitoring, auditing, or security enforcement.

    • Do not abuse AOP for business logic — keep aspects clean and technical, otherwise code becomes hard to trace.

    • Prefer @Around advice when you need maximum control (timing, exception handling, altering return values).

    • For transactions, let Spring’s built-in @Transactional handle it (powered by AOP internally). Don’t reimplement transaction aspects yourself.

  4. Exam Tip

    • If the question mentions join points, pointcuts, advice, weaving, aspects, the answer is always Spring AOP.

    • If it mentions repositories, that’s Spring Data JPA.

    • If it mentions controllers/REST, that’s Spring MVC.

    • If it mentions security enforcement with AOP inside, that’s Spring Security, but the underlying concept remains Spring AOP.

Suggestion: In production projects, rely on Spring AOP only for infrastructural concerns. For business logic, prefer explicit service code. Keep aspects small, focused, and limited to concerns like logging, monitoring, or enforcing technical policies — this ensures your system remains maintainable and transparent.

results.question_plain: Your application contains join points, pointcuts, and advice. What Spring module or feature are you using?

prompt.question:
Given the following property files:

application.properties contains server.port=8181

application-default.properties contains server.port=8282

application-exam.properties contains server.port=8383


If the active profile is 'exam', what will be the value of the server.port property when accessed using @Value("${server.port}")?
feedbacks: Incorrect, profile-specific files override `application.properties` when a profile is active.
Incorrect, `application-default.properties` applies only when no other profile is active.
Correct, `application-exam.properties` matches the active `exam` profile.
Incorrect, the property resolution order is well defined, so the value can be determined.
prompt.explanation:
Spring Boot uses a well-defined property resolution order to determine which configuration values are applied at runtime. This is especially important when dealing with profile-specific property files.

  1. application.properties

    • Always loaded, forms the baseline configuration.

    • Example: server.port=8181 applies unless overridden.

  2. application-default.properties

    • Used only when no active profile is set.

    • Example: server.port=8282 would apply if no profile is active.

  3. application-{profile}.properties

    • Loaded when a profile is explicitly active (via spring.profiles.active).

    • In this case, application-exam.properties contains server.port=8383, and since the active profile is exam, this overrides other values.

  4. Resolution Order in This Scenario

    • application.properties → baseline (8181).

    • application-default.properties → ignored because a profile is active.

    • application-exam.properties → takes precedence because exam is the active profile.

    Final resolved value = 8383.

  5. Best Practices

    • Use application.properties for shared defaults across all profiles.

    • Use application-{profile}.properties for environment-specific overrides (dev, test, prod, exam).

    • Avoid mixing profile logic inside a single file — keep configurations clean and separated.

    • For complex setups, prefer YAML (application.yml) with profile sections for readability.

  6. Exam Tip

    • If the question involves multiple property files, remember:

      • application-{profile}.properties wins if its profile is active.

      • application-default.properties applies only when no profile is active.

      • application.properties is always loaded but can be overridden.

Suggestion: In real projects, always separate environment-specific settings into application-{profile}.properties (e.g., application-dev.properties, application-prod.properties). Keep application.properties minimal and profile-agnostic. This keeps deployments predictable and avoids surprises.

prompt.question: Which of the following statements about the @Autowired annotation in Spring are correct?

Correct. Setter methods can be annotated with @Autowired for method-level dependency injection, where Spring automatically calls the method and injects all its parameters from the application context.
Incorrect, classes require a stereotype such as `@Component`; `@Autowired` is not a class-level marker.
Correct, field injection is supported (although constructor injection is preferred).
prompt.explanation:
@Autowired is Spring’s primary annotation for dependency injection (DI). It tells the container where and how a bean dependency should be injected. Understanding the different injection points and best practices is essential.

  1. Constructor Injection

    • You can annotate a single constructor with @Autowired.

    • If the class has only one constructor, the annotation is optional (Spring will use it automatically).

    • Example:

      @Component
class OrderService {
    private final PaymentService paymentService;

    @Autowired
    public OrderService(PaymentService paymentService) {
        this.paymentService = paymentService;
    }
}

    • ✅ Best practice: always prefer constructor injection → makes dependencies explicit, supports immutability, and is test-friendly.

  2. Method Injection

    • You can annotate a setter method or even specific method parameters with @Autowired.

    • Example:

      @Autowired
public void setDiscountService(DiscountService discountService) { ... }

    • Less recommended because it allows beans to be mutable after construction.

  3. Field Injection

    • Directly annotate a field with @Autowired.

    • Example:

      @Autowired
private UserRepository userRepository;

    • Works but is not recommended for production code: makes unit testing harder, hides dependencies, and prevents immutability.

  4. Class-level Misuse

    • @Autowired cannot be applied at the class level.

    • To make a class a candidate for DI, use stereotypes like @Component, @Service, or @Repository.

  5. Best Practices & Recommendations

    • Constructor injection is the industry standard and exam-safe answer.

    • Field injection is acceptable in quick demos or tests, but avoid in enterprise projects.

    • Use @Qualifier or @Primary with @Autowired when multiple beans of the same type exist.

    • Combine with optional injection:

      @Autowired(required = false)
private SomeOptionalBean optionalBean;

  6. Exam Tip

    • Correct statements:

      • Only one constructor can be annotated with @Autowired.

      • Method arguments can be annotated.

      • Fields can be annotated.

    • Wrong statements:

      • Multiple constructors with @Autowired → not allowed.

      • Using @Autowired at class level instead of @Component → incorrect.

Suggestion: In real-world systems, standardize on constructor injection. It’s explicit, immutable, and plays well with testing and frameworks like Lombok (@RequiredArgsConstructor). Use field or setter injection only in special cases (legacy beans, circular dependencies, or test code).
answers: Multiple constructors in a class can be annotated with @Autowired
Just a single constructor in a class can be annotated with @Autowired
A class can be annotated with @Autowired, instead of @Component
Fields can be annotated with @Autowired for dependency injection

results.question_plain: Which of the following statements about the @Autowired annotation in Spring are correct?

prompt.question: Using the default proxy-based configuration in Spring, which methods will be affected when adding the @Transactional annotation to a class?
feedbacks: Correct, Spring’s proxy-based AOP only advises public methods by default.
Incorrect, protected methods are not proxied unless using AspectJ or class-based proxies with explicit configuration.
Incorrect, private methods are never advised via proxies.
Incorrect, `@Transactional` can also be declared on classes.
Incorrect, final methods cannot be advised because proxies cannot override them.
prompt.explanation:
Spring’s @Transactional is implemented using AOP proxies by default. Understanding which methods are actually intercepted by these proxies is crucial for correctly applying transaction boundaries.

  1. Proxy-based AOP in Spring

    • By default, Spring uses dynamic proxies (JDK proxies for interfaces, or CGLIB proxies for classes).

    • These proxies wrap the bean and intercept method calls, applying cross-cutting logic (like transaction handling).

    • Because of how proxies work, only certain methods can be intercepted.

  2. Which Methods Are Affected?

    • ✅ Public methods → Only public methods are proxied by default. If you annotate a class with @Transactional, all its public methods become transactional.

    • ❌ Protected, private, or package-private methods → Not proxied, so transactions will not apply.

    • ❌ Final methods → Not proxied, since proxies cannot override final methods.

    Example:

    @Service
@Transactional
public class UserService {
    public void saveUser(User u) { ... }      // Transactional
    protected void helperMethod() { ... }     // NOT transactional
    private void validate(User u) { ... }     // NOT transactional
    public final void alwaysRun() { ... }     // NOT transactional
}

  3. Class-Level vs Method-Level Annotation

    • @Transactional can be applied at class level (applies to all public methods) or method level (applies to that method only).

    • Class-level is a common practice for service beans, with overrides at method-level if necessary.

  4. Best Practices

    • Always put @Transactional at the service layer, not the repository layer.

    • Do not rely on private/protected methods being transactional — extract them into separate beans if necessary.

    • For advanced needs (like advising protected/private/final methods), use AspectJ compile-time or load-time weaving, not proxy-based AOP.

  5. Exam Tip

    • With default Spring proxy configuration, only public methods are affected by @Transactional.

    • If you see options mentioning protected/private/final methods, those are incorrect unless the question explicitly says AspectJ weaving.

Suggestion: In real-world projects, keep all transactional boundaries at public service methods. If you need transactional behavior in helper methods, move them to another Spring-managed bean rather than relying on internal self-invocation.
answers: @Transactional may be used only on methods

results.question_plain: Using the default proxy-based configuration in Spring, which methods will be affected when adding the @Transactional annotation to a class?

prompt.question: In Hibernate 5, which of the following strategies is used by default when generating primary key values for an entity annotated with @GeneratedValue and no specific strategy is explicitly defined?
feedbacks: Correct, JPA defaults to `GenerationType.AUTO`, which Hibernate maps to its `SequenceStyleGenerator`.
Incorrect, IDENTITY is only chosen when specified or when AUTO resolves to it for the dialect.
Incorrect, Hibernate may use sequences under the hood, but the declared strategy is AUTO.
Incorrect, TABLE generation must be opted into.
Incorrect, UUID generation requires explicit configuration.
prompt.explanation:
In JPA, when you annotate an entity with @GeneratedValue but don’t specify a strategy, the default is:

@GeneratedValue
private Long id;

This implicitly means @GeneratedValue(strategy = GenerationType.AUTO).

  1. GenerationType.AUTO (Correct Answer)

    • Tells the persistence provider (Hibernate) to choose the appropriate strategy based on the database dialect.

    • In Hibernate 5, AUTO maps to SequenceStyleGenerator, which is flexible enough to use:

      • A SEQUENCE if the database supports it (e.g., PostgreSQL, Oracle).

      • An IDENTITY if sequences aren’t supported (e.g., MySQL).

      • A TABLE generator fallback if neither sequence nor identity is supported.

  2. Other Strategies

    • IDENTITY → Directly uses auto-increment/identity columns. Useful in MySQL.

    • SEQUENCE → Uses database sequences. Requires explicit choice or database support.

    • TABLE → Simulates sequences using a separate table. Rarely used today due to inefficiency.

    • UUID → Hibernate-specific, requires explicit configuration. Not part of JPA standard.

  3. Why AUTO is Useful

    • Provides portability across different databases.

    • You don’t need to hardcode strategy per vendor.

    • Hibernate adapts automatically via the dialect.

  4. Best Practices

    • Use AUTO in projects that must support multiple DBs.

    • If tied to a specific DB (e.g., PostgreSQL), prefer SEQUENCE for efficiency.

    • Avoid TABLE unless working with a DB without identity/sequence support.

    • For distributed systems needing global uniqueness, use explicit UUID generators.

  5. Exam Tip

    • Default with no strategy specified = AUTO.

    • Hibernate 5 maps AUTO → SequenceStyleGenerator → best available strategy per DB.

Suggestion: In production, if you know your database supports sequences (like PostgreSQL or Oracle), explicitly declare SEQUENCE for efficiency. Use AUTO only when you need vendor portability.

results.question_plain: In Hibernate 5, which of the following strategies is used by default when generating primary key values for an entity annotated with @GeneratedValue and no specific strategy is explicitly defined?

prompt.question: When is setter injection the most appropriate choice in Spring Dependency Injection?
feedbacks: Correct, setter injection keeps beans mutable for later reconfiguration.
Incorrect, immutable beans should favour constructor injection.
Correct, setter injection can be used when a third-party class lacks an injectable constructor.
Incorrect, serialization requirements do not dictate setter injection.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring, Dependency Injection (DI) can be done via constructor injection or setter injection. Constructor injection is generally preferred, but setter injection still has valid use cases.

  1. When Setter Injection is Appropriate

    • ✅ Beans that require later reconfiguration or mutability

      • Setter injection keeps the bean mutable, allowing dependencies or properties to be reassigned after construction.

      • Useful for cases where configuration changes dynamically (e.g., swapping strategies at runtime).

    • ✅ Legacy or third-party classes without injectable constructors

      • Some classes may not expose a constructor suitable for DI. In these cases, Spring can instantiate the bean with the default constructor and inject collaborators via setters.

  2. When Setter Injection is NOT Appropriate

    • ❌ Immutable beans → Constructor injection is better, as it enforces required dependencies and immutability.

    • ❌ Serialization needs → Having a no-arg constructor for frameworks like JPA or Jackson doesn’t imply setter injection; you can still use constructor injection for mandatory dependencies.

  3. Best Practices

    • Default to constructor injection → it makes dependencies explicit, enforces immutability, and supports easier testing.

    • Use setter injection only for optional or changeable dependencies, or when forced by legacy APIs.

    • Avoid mixing both types excessively — it makes code harder to reason about.

  4. Exam Tip

    • Correct answers:

      • Setter injection for beans that need reconfiguration.

      • Setter injection for legacy/third-party code without suitable constructors.

    • Incorrect answers involve immutability or serialization.

Suggestion: In production, design all new beans with constructor injection. Use setter injection only for optional dependencies or when working with external libraries where you can’t change constructors. This keeps your system more robust and testable.
answers: when the created bean must support further reconfiguration
when creating an immutable bean that depends on another bean
when the type of bean being created does not support other types of injection (e.g.: legacy or third party code)
when the bean requires a default constructor for serialization purposes

results.question_plain: When is setter injection the most appropriate choice in Spring Dependency Injection?

prompt.question: Fill in the blanks. Select the one that applies best. REST is ______, and usually uses the ______ protocol.
feedbacks: Incorrect, REST must be stateless.
Incorrect, REST is the architectural style, not the transport protocol.
Incorrect, REST is stateless but does not use REST as a protocol.
Correct, REST is stateless and most commonly uses HTTP.
Incorrect, REST is not stateful and is not bound to raw TCP.
prompt.explanation:
REST (Representational State Transfer) is an architectural style for building distributed systems. Its two key characteristics relevant here are statelessness and protocol usage.

  1. Statelessness (Correct)

    • Every request from a client to a server must contain all information needed to process it.

    • The server does not store any client session state between requests.

    • This makes REST scalable, as any request can be handled by any server in a cluster.

    • Example: Authentication via tokens (JWT) instead of server-side session state.

  2. Protocol (Usually HTTP)

    • REST is protocol-agnostic in theory, but in practice it almost always uses HTTP.

    • HTTP methods map naturally to CRUD operations:

      • GET → Read

      • POST → Create

      • PUT → Replace

      • PATCH → Update (partial)

      • DELETE → Remove

    • REST also uses HTTP status codes and content negotiation (JSON, XML, etc.).

  3. Other Protocols (Rare)

    • REST can be built over other protocols (e.g., HTTPS, WebSockets, or even message queues), but HTTP is by far the most common and exam-relevant.

  4. Best Practices

    • Always design REST APIs to be stateless.

    • Avoid storing session data server-side; use tokens, headers, or cookies with self-contained data.

    • Use proper HTTP methods and status codes for predictable, standard behavior.

  5. Exam Tip

    • REST is stateless.

    • REST usually uses HTTP as its transport protocol.

    • If you see "stateful" in options, that’s always wrong for REST.

results.question_plain: Fill in the blanks. Select the one that applies best. REST is ______, and usually uses the ______ protocol.

prompt.question: Which of the following can be used to execute SQL scripts before running test methods in Spring?
feedbacks: Correct, `ResourceDatabasePopulator` can load scripts before tests via `addScripts`.
Incorrect, there is no `@SqlScript` annotation; use `@Sql` instead.
Incorrect, `SqlRunner` is not part of Spring’s testing support.
Correct, `@Sql` executes scripts before or after test methods.
Incorrect, `JdbcTemplate` exposes query/update operations, not a bulk `executeScripts` helper.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring’s testing framework, you often need to initialize or reset the database state before running tests. Spring provides dedicated mechanisms for this, both programmatic and declarative.

  1. @Sql Annotation (Correct)

    • Declarative way to execute SQL scripts before or after test methods.

    • Can be applied at the class or method level.

    • Example:

      @Test
@Sql(scripts = "/test-data.sql")
public void testUserRepository() {
    // Executes with DB pre-populated from test-data.sql
}

    • Supports executionPhase to run before or after tests:

      @Sql(scripts = "/cleanup.sql", executionPhase = Sql.ExecutionPhase.AFTER_TEST_METHOD)

  2. ResourceDatabasePopulator.addScripts (Correct)

    • Programmatic way to populate the database with SQL scripts.

    • Useful when you want explicit control within the test setup.

    • Example:

      ResourceDatabasePopulator populator = new ResourceDatabasePopulator();
populator.addScripts(new ClassPathResource("schema.sql"), new ClassPathResource("data.sql"));
DatabasePopulatorUtils.execute(populator, dataSource);

  3. Incorrect Options

    • @SqlScript → ❌ No such annotation in Spring (trap option).

    • SqlRunner.runScript → ❌ Not part of Spring test utilities.

    • JdbcTemplate.executeScripts → ❌ JdbcTemplate doesn’t expose this method; it executes single SQL statements via execute, query, etc.

  4. Best Practices

    • Use @Sql for simple integration tests where you want repeatable setup/cleanup across multiple test runs.

    • Use ResourceDatabasePopulator if you need programmatic control (e.g., conditional loading of scripts).

    • For bigger projects, consider DbUnit or testcontainers for more robust test DB management.

  5. Exam Tip

    • If the question is about annotations, answer is @Sql.

    • If the question is about programmatic API, answer is ResourceDatabasePopulator.addScripts.

Suggestion: In production-grade integration tests, prefer @Sql for readability and standardization. Use ResourceDatabasePopulator only when you need fine-grained, programmatic setup beyond what @Sql provides.
answers: addScripts method of the ResourceDatabasePopulator class
runScript method of the SqlRunner class

results.question_plain: Which of the following can be used to execute SQL scripts before running test methods in Spring?

prompt.question: Which of the following advices in Spring AOP can execute code after a join point throws an exception?
feedbacks: Incorrect, `@AfterReturning` runs only on successful completion.
Correct, `@AfterThrowing` is triggered when the join point throws an exception.
Correct, `@Around` advice can wrap the invocation and react when an exception is thrown.
Incorrect, `@After` executes in a finally-style manner regardless of outcome.
Incorrect, `@Before` runs prior to the method.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring AOP, advices define when cross-cutting logic should run relative to the execution of a join point (usually a method). To handle exceptions, you must know which advices are designed for error scenarios.

  1. @AfterThrowing (Correct)

    • Runs only if the target method throws an exception.

    • Allows you to log, wrap, or handle exceptions in a centralized way.

    • Example:

      @AfterThrowing(pointcut = "execution(* com.example.service.*.*(..))", throwing = "ex")
public void logException(Exception ex) {
    logger.error("Exception caught: {}", ex.getMessage());
}

  2. @Around (Correct)

    • Surrounds the method execution.

    • Can handle both normal execution and exceptions because it has direct control over proceed().

    • Most powerful advice, but also most complex.

    • Example:

      @Around("execution(* com.example.service.*.*(..))")
public Object wrapCall(ProceedingJoinPoint pjp) throws Throwable {
    try {
        return pjp.proceed();
    } catch (Throwable ex) {
        logger.error("Exception: {}", ex.getMessage());
        throw ex;
    }
}

  3. Other Advices (Not Exception-Specific)

    • @Before → Runs before the method starts, never handles exceptions.

    • @AfterReturning → Runs only on success, skipped if an exception occurs.

    • @After → Runs regardless of success or failure (finally semantics), but doesn’t give direct access to the exception.

  4. Best Practices

    • Use @AfterThrowing for centralized logging/monitoring of exceptions.

    • Use @Around when you need full control (e.g., measuring execution time, retry logic, exception translation).

    • Avoid overusing exception advices for business logic — handle domain-specific exceptions inside services.

  5. Exam Tip

    • If the question asks specifically about exceptions → @AfterThrowing.

    • If it asks for flexibility with exceptions and normal flow → @Around.

Suggestion: In production systems, rely on @AfterThrowing for cross-cutting error handling (logging, metrics) and reserve @Around for advanced concerns like retries, transactions, or global performance measurement.

results.question_plain: Which of the following advices in Spring AOP can execute code after a join point throws an exception?

prompt.question: Which of the following methods can be used to disable a specific auto-configuration class in Spring Boot?
feedbacks: Correct, use the `exclude` attribute to disable specific auto-configurations.

Incorrect. @DisableAutoConfiguration does not exist in Spring Boot.
The correct way to disable auto-configuration is to use @EnableAutoConfiguration with the exclude attribute.
Incorrect, the property key is `spring.autoconfigure.exclude`.
Incorrect, `@Disable` is not a Spring Boot annotation.
prompt.explanation:
Spring Boot’s auto-configuration mechanism loads classes automatically based on the classpath and environment. However, sometimes you need to disable a specific auto-configuration (e.g., to replace it with a custom bean or prevent unwanted behavior).

  1. Disabling Auto-Configuration via Annotation

    • The primary mechanism is using the exclude attribute of @EnableAutoConfiguration or @SpringBootApplication:

      @SpringBootApplication(exclude = { DataSourceAutoConfiguration.class })
public class MyApp {}

    • This ensures the specified auto-configuration class will not be applied.

    • ✅ Exam-safe answer.

  2. Disabling Auto-Configuration via Properties

    • In application.properties or application.yml, use:

      spring.autoconfigure.exclude=org.springframework.boot.autoconfigure.jdbc.DataSourceAutoConfiguration

    • Useful when you want to disable auto-configurations without touching source code (e.g., across different environments).

  3. Invalid or Misleading Options

    • excludeNames={ClassName.class} → ❌ Wrong type, it accepts string class names, not literals.

    • spring.configure.exclude → ❌ Incorrect property key; correct is spring.autoconfigure.exclude.

    • @Disable → ❌ Not part of Spring Boot.

  4. Best Practices

    • Use the annotation-based exclude when you need fine-grained control at the application level.

    • Use the property-based exclusion when working across multiple environments (so you don’t hardcode exclusions in code).

    • Avoid disabling auto-config lightly; most of the time, Spring Boot’s conditional beans allow you to override specific beans without disabling an entire configuration.

  5. Exam Tip

    • If asked about annotation-based disabling → @EnableAutoConfiguration(exclude={ClassName.class}).

    • If asked about property-based disabling → spring.autoconfigure.exclude.

Suggestion: In production, prefer overriding or providing your own beans before excluding auto-configuration. Only disable an auto-config when you’re sure it conflicts with your design, otherwise you risk breaking other Boot features that depend on it.

results.question_plain: Which of the following methods can be used to disable a specific auto-configuration class in Spring Boot?

prompt.question:
Which of the following best matches the description:

'An architectural style for designing distributed applications, which defines a set of constraints on how the application should behave to access and manipulate resources'?
feedbacks: Incorrect, SOAP is a messaging protocol, not an architectural style.
Correct, REST is an architectural style defined by a set of constraints.
Incorrect, MVC is a design pattern for user interfaces.
Incorrect, OOP is a programming paradigm.
prompt.explanation:
The description clearly refers to REST (Representational State Transfer). Unlike a protocol, REST is an architectural style defined by a set of constraints for building distributed systems.

  1. REST Characteristics

    • Statelessness → Each request must contain all required information; server does not maintain client session state.

    • Client-Server Separation → Clear separation of concerns between client and server.

    • Uniform Interface → Standardized resource access through URIs, HTTP methods (GET, POST, PUT, DELETE, PATCH), and status codes.

    • Cacheability → Responses can be cached to improve scalability.

    • Layered System → Architecture can include intermediaries like load balancers or proxies.

    • Optional Code-on-Demand → Servers can extend client functionality (rarely used in practice).

  2. REST vs Other Options

    • SOAP → Protocol with rigid XML-based messaging format. Not an architectural style.

    • MVC → A UI architectural pattern separating Model, View, and Controller, unrelated to distributed system constraints.

    • OOP → Programming paradigm (objects, encapsulation, inheritance), not a distributed system architecture.

  3. Best Practices in REST

    • Use HTTP methods semantically (GET for read, POST for create, PUT for replace, PATCH for update, DELETE for remove).

    • Design URIs to represent resources, not actions (e.g., /users/123 instead of /getUser).

    • Leverage HTTP status codes correctly (200, 201, 400, 404, 500, etc.).

    • Keep APIs stateless and idempotent where applicable.

  4. Exam Tip

    • If the question mentions “architectural style with constraints” → always REST.

    • If it mentions protocol with strict XML rules → SOAP.

    • If it mentions UI separation → MVC.

    • If it mentions encapsulation and objects → OOP.

results.question_plain: Which of the following best matches the description: 'An architectural style for designing distributed applications, which defines a set of constraints on how the application should behave to access and manipulate resources'?

prompt.question: Fill in the blank. In Spring MVC, some of the values returned by _____________ are: a String, a View, or a ModelAndView.
feedbacks: Incorrect, view resolvers themselves do not return these types.
Incorrect, `HandlerMapping` resolves handlers; it does not return view representations.
Correct, controller handler methods may return `String`, `View`, or `ModelAndView`.
Incorrect, `Model` methods add attributes rather than return values.
Incorrect, `DispatcherServlet` coordinates processing but does not return these values.
prompt.explanation:
In Spring MVC, the return value of a controller handler method determines how the DispatcherServlet processes the response. Handler methods are the entry point of controller logic, and depending on what they return, Spring decides how to resolve and render the view.

  1. Valid Return Types for Controller Methods

    • String → Represents the logical view name (e.g., "home" → resolved by a ViewResolver to an actual JSP, Thymeleaf template, etc.).

    • ModelAndView → Bundles both the model data and view name/object in one return type.

    • View → Directly returns a View implementation (e.g., MappingJackson2JsonView for JSON).

    • void → Can be used if the method writes directly to the response or uses @ResponseBody.

    • @ResponseBody / ResponseEntity → For REST controllers, returning domain objects or responses serialized as JSON/XML.

  2. Other Participants in MVC Flow

    • DispatcherServlet orchestrates request handling but doesn’t return a view.

    • HandlerMapping maps incoming requests to handler methods.

    • ViewResolver translates view names (like "home") into concrete view objects, but doesn’t return them directly from controllers.

  3. Best Practices

    • Use String for simple MVC views when you just need to return a view name.

    • Use ModelAndView when you want to return both data and view in one step.

    • Use @ResponseBody or ResponseEntity for REST endpoints (modern style).

    • Keep controllers lean: focus on orchestrating flow, not embedding business logic.

  4. Exam Tip

    • If the question involves returning String, View, or ModelAndView → the answer is controller handler methods.

    • DispatcherServlet, HandlerMapping, and ViewResolvers handle orchestration, not return values.

Suggestion: In production REST APIs, avoid ModelAndView. Use @RestController with ResponseEntity for clean and testable endpoints. Reserve String or ModelAndView for legacy MVC template-based apps.

results.question_plain: Fill in the blank. In Spring MVC, some of the values returned by _____________ are: a String, a View, or a ModelAndView.

prompt.question: Which component acts as the front controller in Spring MVC-based applications?
feedbacks: Incorrect, controllers handle specific mappings but are not the global entry point.
Incorrect, `FrontController` is not a Spring component.
Correct, `DispatcherServlet` receives every request and dispatches it to handlers.
Incorrect, `FrontDispatcherController` is not part of Spring MVC.
Incorrect, `RequestHandlerServlet` is not the Spring MVC front controller.
prompt.explanation:
In the Spring MVC architecture, the Front Controller design pattern is implemented by the DispatcherServlet. It is the central entry point for all incoming HTTP requests in a Spring MVC application.

  1. Role of DispatcherServlet

    • Intercepts all incoming requests mapped in web.xml (legacy) or automatically configured in Spring Boot.

    • Delegates request handling to appropriate components (controllers, view resolvers, exception handlers).

    • Orchestrates the entire MVC flow:

      • Receives the request.

      • Finds the appropriate handler/controller using HandlerMapping.

      • Executes the controller method.

      • Resolves the view using ViewResolver.

      • Renders the response back to the client.

  2. Why Not Other Options?

    • Controller → Handles only mapped requests, not all incoming traffic.

    • FrontController / FrontDispatcherController / RequestHandlerServlet → Not real Spring components (trap options).

    • Only DispatcherServlet implements the Front Controller pattern in Spring MVC.

  3. Best Practices

    • In Spring Boot, DispatcherServlet is auto-registered at the root path (/) and requires no explicit configuration.

    • For legacy apps, ensure it is defined in web.xml or via ServletRegistrationBean.

    • Keep controllers focused on business logic mapping, let DispatcherServlet manage orchestration.

  4. Exam Tip

    • Whenever you see "Front Controller" in the context of Spring MVC → the answer is DispatcherServlet.

results.question_plain: Which component acts as the front controller in Spring MVC-based applications?

prompt.question: Which of the following statements are correct regarding Spring AOP?
feedbacks: Correct, Spring AOP addresses cross-cutting concerns such as transactions or logging.
Correct, proxy-based AOP cannot override final methods.

Incorrect, Spring AOP uses AspectJ's annotation syntax (@Aspect, @Before, @After, @Around, @Pointcut) rather than defining its own proprietary annotation set. Spring implements these AspectJ annotations using its proxy-based runtime weaving mechanism.
Correct, Spring chooses JDK dynamic proxies when interfaces are present.
Incorrect, compile-time weaving is offered by AspectJ, not proxy-based Spring AOP.
prompt.explanation:
Spring AOP (Aspect-Oriented Programming) provides a proxy-based mechanism for applying cross-cutting concerns, such as transactions, logging, caching, security, without polluting business logic. It integrates with Spring’s IoC container and uses runtime proxies to wrap beans.

  1. Cross-Cutting Concerns (Correct)

    • AOP modularizes logic that is spread across multiple classes (e.g., logging, transactions, security).

    • Example: @Transactional is implemented internally with AOP proxies.

  2. Proxy Limitations (Correct)

    • Spring AOP is proxy-based, not full bytecode weaving.

    • Proxies cannot override:

      • final methods → No interception possible.

      • private methods → Not proxied.

    • Only public methods are advised by default.

  3. Annotations (Incorrect Statement Clarification)

    • Spring defines its own AOP annotations, mainly via the @Aspect API from spring-aspects, which is based on AspectJ’s semantics.

    • Example: @Aspect, @Before, @AfterReturning, @Around.

    • While syntax comes from AspectJ, Spring provides its own runtime proxy-based implementation — so saying "Spring only uses AspectJ annotations" is misleading.

  4. Proxy Types (Correct)

    • By default, if a bean implements at least one interface, JDK Dynamic Proxy is used.

    • If the bean has no interfaces, Spring falls back to CGLIB proxies.

    • Example:

      • Interface present → JDK Proxy.

      • No interface → CGLIB subclass proxy.

  5. Weaving Model (Incorrect Statement Clarification)

    • Spring AOP supports only runtime weaving using proxies.

    • AspectJ supports compile-time, post-compile, and load-time weaving, which is more powerful but requires extra setup.

  6. Best Practices

    • Use AOP for technical cross-cutting logic, not for core business rules.

    • Stick with proxy-based AOP unless you truly need AspectJ-level power (e.g., field interception, non-public method weaving).

    • Always remember proxy limitations when designing transactional or security boundaries.

  7. Exam Tip

    • Correct statements:

      • Spring AOP solves cross-cutting concerns.

      • It cannot proxy final methods.

      • It uses JDK Dynamic Proxies by default.

    • Incorrect statements:

      • "Spring uses AspectJ annotations only" → misleading, Spring provides its own.

      • "Spring AOP applies aspects at compile time" → false, that’s AspectJ.

Suggestion: In production, design service beans with interfaces — this makes proxying predictable and aligns with best practices (clean layering, easier mocking in tests). Use AspectJ only if you hit Spring AOP’s proxy limitations.
answers: Spring uses AOP to implement solutions for cross-cutting concerns
Spring AOP cannot proxy final methods
Spring AOP uses its own proprietary annotations and does not support AspectJ annotations

prompt.question: Can the @Bean annotation be used together with the @Profile annotation in Spring Framework?
feedbacks: Correct, `@Bean` and `@Profile` are often combined to register profile-specific beans.
Incorrect, Spring does not forbid combining these annotations.
prompt.explanation:
This is a common and recommended practice when you want to register beans only for specific environments (profiles).

  1. How It Works

    • @Bean → Declares a bean method inside a @Configuration class.

    • @Profile → Restricts the bean’s registration to specific active profiles.

    • Together, this means Spring will only create that bean when the matching profile is active.

    Example:

    @Configuration
public class DataSourceConfig {

    @Bean
    @Profile("dev")
    public DataSource devDataSource() {
        return new HikariDataSource(devConfig());
    }

    @Bean
    @Profile("prod")
    public DataSource prodDataSource() {
        return new HikariDataSource(prodConfig());
    }
}

    • If spring.profiles.active=dev, only devDataSource is registered.

    • If spring.profiles.active=prod, only prodDataSource is registered.

  2. Alternative Usage

    • @Profile can also be applied at the class level to include/exclude an entire @Configuration class based on active profiles.

  3. Best Practices

    • Use @Profile to cleanly separate environment-specific beans (dev, test, staging, prod).

    • Keep environment differences in configuration classes rather than scattering if conditions across your code.

    • Use application-{profile}.properties for configuration differences and @Profile for bean wiring differences.

prompt.question: JdbcTemplate in Spring Framework is an example of which design pattern?
feedbacks: Correct, `JdbcTemplate` implements the Template Method pattern to wrap boilerplate JDBC code.
Incorrect, `JdbcTemplate` does not adapt one interface to another.
Incorrect, it does not act as a factory for other objects.
Incorrect, `JdbcTemplate` does not decorate another implementation.
Incorrect, it is not inherently a singleton; you may define multiple instances.
prompt.explanation:
JdbcTemplate in Spring Framework is a textbook example of the Template Method design pattern.

  1. Why Template Method?

    • JDBC code is notoriously verbose: opening connections, preparing statements, handling exceptions, closing resources.

    • The invariant part of the workflow (connection management, exception translation, resource cleanup) is implemented by JdbcTemplate.

    • The variable part (the actual SQL, parameters, and result set mapping) is delegated to callbacks such as RowMapper, ResultSetExtractor, or PreparedStatementSetter.

    • This is exactly how the Template Method pattern works: a fixed algorithm skeleton with pluggable steps provided by the client.

    Example:

    List<User> users = jdbcTemplate.query(
    "SELECT id, name FROM users",
    (rs, rowNum) -> new User(rs.getLong("id"), rs.getString("name"))
);

    • The template handles connection lifecycle, exceptions, closing resources.

    • You only implement the "mapping" step.

  2. Why Not Other Patterns?

    • Adapter → Adapts one interface to another (not the case here).

    • Factory → Creates objects (not the case, JdbcTemplate executes logic).

    • Decorator → Wraps functionality without changing interface (not the case).

    • Singleton → A scope/lifecycle concept, not the inherent pattern of JdbcTemplate.

  3. Best Practices

    • Always use JdbcTemplate instead of raw JDBC for better readability, safety, and exception translation.

    • Use NamedParameterJdbcTemplate for improved readability when dealing with multiple query parameters.

    • Prefer Spring Data JPA for complex persistence needs, but fall back to JdbcTemplate when you need direct SQL control.

results.question_plain: JdbcTemplate in Spring Framework is an example of which design pattern?

prompt.question: Which of the following URLs is used to access the available actuator endpoints in a Spring Boot application with the default configuration? Assume the application is running on port 8080.
feedbacks: Correct, the actuator endpoints are exposed under `/actuator` by default.
Incorrect, Spring Boot does not create a `/bootactuator` path.
Incorrect, `/boot/actuator` is not the default context path.
Incorrect, there is no `/endpoint/actuator` base path in the default setup.
prompt.explanation:
Spring Boot provides the Actuator module for exposing operational endpoints — things like health checks, metrics, environment info, thread dumps, and more. These endpoints are extremely useful for monitoring and managing applications in production.

  1. Default Base Path

    • By default, all actuator endpoints are exposed under:

      http://localhost:8080/actuator

    • This base path is configurable via:

      management.endpoints.web.base-path=/manage

      If you change it, endpoints will be available under /manage instead of /actuator.

  2. Available Endpoints

    • With the starter added (spring-boot-starter-actuator), endpoints are auto-configured.

    • Examples:

      • /actuator/health → Health status (exposed by default).

      • /actuator/info → Application info (metadata, version, build info).

      • /actuator/metrics → Metrics (memory, threads, custom metrics).

      • /actuator/env → Environment properties.

  3. Exposure Settings

    • By default, only /health and /info are exposed over HTTP.

    • To expose more endpoints:

      management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=*

    • Or restrict explicitly:

      management.endpoints.web.exposure.include=health,info,metrics
management.endpoints.web.exposure.exclude=env

  4. Security Considerations

    • Actuator endpoints may expose sensitive information.

    • In production, secure them with Spring Security and limit exposure to trusted networks.

  5. Exam Tip

    • Default URL → http://localhost:8080/actuator.

    • If a question mentions /bootactuator, /boot/actuator, or /endpoint/actuator, those are wrong.

    • Always remember: base path = /actuator, unless changed in properties.

Suggestion: In production, expose only necessary actuator endpoints (like health and info), secure them via Spring Security, and integrate with monitoring tools (Prometheus, Grafana, ELK).
